Mayor Contact Ed Hooks Mayor (919) 880-9146 ehooks@cityofmebane.com 106 E. Washington Street Mebane, NC 27302 A letter from the Mayor Come experience Mebane, North Carolina’s most vibrant community. We enthusiastically welcome new businesses and residents with excellent amenities and affordable properties. Mebane is ideally located in North Carolina’s Piedmont region, midway between the majestic Blue Ridge Mountains and the state’s beautiful barrier island coastline. With two interstate highways running through our city and a number of nationally known universities and medical center close by, we offer an ideal environment for our highly diversified business community. Residents of Mebane enjoy an abundance of recreational opportunities and public schools that rate highly in state scoring data. Our growth in new housing continues, providing citizens a wide range of residential options. We hope you will enjoy exploring all that our positively charming city has to offer. Cordially, Ed Hooks

City Council Council meetings Members Live Feed Back 2020 meeting archive 2021 meeting archive 2022 meeting archive 2023 meeting archive 2024 meeting archive 2025 Meeting Archive Back Boards and Commissions About Mebane has a City Council and City Manager form of government. Similarly, both Alamance and Orange Counties have a Board of Commissioners with a County Manager. Scheduled meetings of the Mebane City Council are held on the first Monday of each month. In the event that the regularly scheduled meeting falls on a City observed holiday, the meeting will be rescheduled for the second Monday of the month. Meetings begin at 6:00 p.m. and are held at: Mebane City Hall - Council Chambers 106 E. Washington Street Mebane, North Carolina 27302 To view the 2026 Council Meeting Calendar, click here .

At said place and time, all Bids that have been duly received will be publicly opened and read aloud.
Bidders must be duly licensed contractors in the State of North Carolina for the appropriate work which is being Bid.

Each Bid shall be accompanied with a deposit of cash, CERTIFIED CHECK or Bid Bond on bank or trust company in an amount equal to not less than five percent (5%) of the Bid Amount for each Bid submitted.

Each Successful Bidder will be required to furnish a Performance Bond and a Payment Bond as security for the faithful performance and the payment of all bids and obligations arising from the performance of the Contract.
Bidders may not withdraw a bid within 60 days after the actual date of the opening thereof. The City reserves the right to reject any or all bids and to waive informalities.

The Request for Proposals can be found at: RFP – Water Tank Asset Maintenance Program

Any firm wishing to be considered for providing the services described for this project shall submit 3 sets of proposals. Send or deliver proposals to the following address on or before 2:00 PM ET on Wednesday, May 20, 2026 in order to be considered. Proposals submitted after the deadline will be rejected. Kyle Smith, Public Utilities Director City of Mebane 106 E Washington Street Mebane, NC 27302
The City of Mebane is soliciting bids for the West Ten Road Waterline Connector. Bid Documents may be obtained from Engineer’s webpage by downloading the documents from:

Prior to accessing files, participants will be required to accept Engineer’s Download Policy. The Policy requires that documents shall not be forwarded to other bidders; however, you will be permitted to forward to material suppliers to obtaining quotes and to subcontractors provided suppliers and subcontractors do not forward the documents to other bidders.
If you prefer to receive a hard copy of the bid documents, please contact Connie Lavance at clavance@awck.com or 336-226-5534 and we will forward you a set or you may come by and pick up a set.

Jun June 20 2026 5:30pm - 8:30pm Mebane Arts and Community Center 633 Corrigedor Dr., Mebane, NC 27302 Share Facebook Twitter Email Directions The 2026 Mebane Sports Hall of Fame Induction Gala is a special evening dedicated to celebrating the rich athletic tradition and community spirit of Mebane. Hosted on June 20, 2026, at the Mebane Arts & Community Center, the gala honors outstanding athletes, coaches, and community leaders whose achievements and contributions have left a lasting impact on local sports history. The evening features a meet-and-greet reception, dinner, keynote address by former professional basketball player and coach Sylvia Crawley-Spann, and the induction ceremonies for this year’s Hall of Honor and Hall of Fame recipients. Guests will gather to recognize the accomplishments of distinguished inductees including BJ Chockley, Terrence Caldwell, Jeff Modlin, Wayne Petty, Doug Smith, Dean Ray, and John Kirby. Presented with the support of local sponsors, partners, and the Mebane Sports Hall of Fame Committee, the gala serves as both a celebration of past achievements and an inspiration for future generations of athletes and community leaders. Tickets $25.00 per person (tables will seat a maximum of 7) Purchase tickets online Visit the Mebane Arts and Community Center during normal business hours. Sports Hall of Fame Inductees John Kirby – John Kirby has spent decades shaping the athletic tradition of Eastern Alamance and the Mebane community as both a standout athlete and one of the area’s most respected coaches. After excelling in football and baseball at Eastern Alamance and continuing his education at East Carolina University, Kirby built a distinguished coaching career marked by conference championships, playoff success, and numerous coaching honors. Beyond the field, his leadership as an athletic director and contributor to athletic organizations has helped strengthen sports development and mentorship opportunities throughout the region. ​ Jeff Modlin – Jeff Modlin’s lifelong impact on Mebane athletics spans more than four decades as an athlete, coach, mentor, and community leader. Beginning as a standout youth athlete and continuing through Eastern Alamance High School and competitive softball, Modlin earned numerous championships and honors while building a reputation for dedication and excellence. As a coach for over 35 years across multiple sports, he has mentored more than 1,000 young athletes and helped guide countless teams to success, while also contributing to the growth of local athletics through service on recreation and community boards. Wayne Petty – Wayne Petty distinguished himself as a leader and accomplished golfer from his days at Eastern Alamance High School through an outstanding collegiate career at Appalachian State University. A multi-sport athlete, Eagle Scout, and team captain, Petty earned numerous honors in golf while helping lead championship teams and serving in prominent campus leadership roles. His success continued in amateur golf with multiple championship titles and national recognition, reflecting a lifelong commitment to excellence, leadership, and community involvement in Mebane athletics. Dean Ray – Dean Ray transformed recreation and athletics in Mebane through more than 30 years of visionary leadership as Director of Recreation and Parks. After earning degrees in recreation while playing collegiate football at Catawba College, Ray dedicated his career to expanding youth sports, developing major community programs and events, and leading the creation of key facilities including the Mebane Arts and Community Center and Mebane Community Park. His commitment to accessibility, participation, and community development left a lasting impact on generations of Mebane residents and athletes. Doug Smith – Doug Smith’s lifelong contribution to football began as a leader and co-captain at Eastern Alamance High School before evolving into a remarkable 49-year officiating career at both the high school and collegiate levels. Widely respected for his professionalism and consistency, Smith officiated major championship games, NCAA playoff contests, and prestigious bowl games including the Sugar Bowl and New Mexico Bowl. His decades of dedication and leadership have left a lasting mark on football and the athletic tradition of the Mebane community. Sports Hall of Honor Inductees BJ Chockley – Betty Jo “BJ” Woodard Chockley built an extraordinary legacy in athletics and sports medicine through decades of service to Eastern Alamance athletics. A standout basketball player at Northampton County High School and UNC, where she earned All-Conference honors and competed in the Women’s NIT, she later became a pioneer in sports medicine coverage for high school athletics. For 45 years, she provided sideline care for Eastern Alamance football and 20 years for wrestling, helping establish one of the premier athletic training programs in the state while mentoring countless students, athletes, and medical professionals. Terrence Caldwell – Terrence Caldwell dedicated nearly four decades to leadership, athletics, and public service through careers in the military, law enforcement, coaching, and mentoring. A standout basketball player at Chapel Hill High School, Caldwell served in the U.S. Army and Military Police Corps before becoming Chief of Police for the City of Mebane, where he served for 17 years. Throughout his career, he remained deeply involved in athletics as a coach, mentor, and sports official, impacting generations of young athletes through his commitment to discipline, leadership, and community service. Keynote speaker Sylvia Crawley Spann – Sylvia Crawley Spann is a Gold Medalist, WNBA legend, NCAA champion, and Best-Selling Author of The Confidence Playbook. She is most known for winning the first ever Female Slam Dunk Contest with a Blindfolded Dunk. As an international speaker and Founder of her SLAM Signature Program, Sylvia ignites athletic and corporate teams to champion their fields, all while leading with faith, family, and unstoppable purpose. Her TED Talk would be on the topic of confidence. Future inductees The Hall of Fame Committee has an ongoing search for potential inductees. If you would like to submit an entry to be considered by the committee you may e-mail the recommendation to Recreation Superintendent Kris Kloepping. Past recipients View previous Sports Hall of Fame recipients. After earning degrees in recreation while playing collegiate football at Catawba College, Ray dedicated his career to expanding youth sports, developing major community programs and events, and leading the creation of key facilities including the Mebane Arts and Community Center and Mebane Community Park. His commitment to accessibility, participation, and community development left a lasting impact on generations of Mebane residents and athletes.

Doug Smith – Doug Smith’s lifelong contribution to football began as a leader and co-captain at Eastern Alamance High School before evolving into a remarkable 49-year officiating career at both the high school and collegiate levels. Widely respected for his professionalism and consistency, Smith officiated major championship games, NCAA playoff contests, and prestigious bowl games including the Sugar Bowl and New Mexico Bowl. His decades of dedication and leadership have left a lasting mark on football and the athletic tradition of the Mebane community.

BJ Chockley – Betty Jo “BJ” Woodard Chockley built an extraordinary legacy in athletics and sports medicine through decades of service to Eastern Alamance athletics. A standout basketball player at Northampton County High School and UNC, where she earned All-Conference honors and competed in the Women’s NIT, she later became a pioneer in sports medicine coverage for high school athletics. For 45 years, she provided sideline care for Eastern Alamance football and 20 years for wrestling, helping establish one of the premier athletic training programs in the state while mentoring countless students, athletes, and medical professionals.

The Hall of Fame Committee has an ongoing search for potential inductees. If you would like to submit an entry to be considered by the committee you may e-mail the recommendation to Recreation Superintendent Kris Kloepping.

About The City of Mebane follows the Council-Manager form of government. Under this form of government, residents elect a Mayor and City Council, who then appoint a professional City Manager to oversee the day-to-day operations of the town. Most cities and counties in North Carolina follow this form of government. City Council members are the community leaders and policy makers who establish a vision for Mebane, and who hire the manager to carry out policy and ensure that all residents are equitably served. The manager coordinates the work of department heads and other employees, who help ensure the smooth and efficient delivery of services. In addition, the manager builds public/private partnerships to target all of a community's resources to creatively solve current problems. Ultimately, the city manager's primary responsibility is to keep our community running smoothly.

Our goal is to provide our service to the community professionally and competently. Our biggest compliment is that our residents just "assume and expect" it will get done. We are proud of our city and will constantly strive to improve it and keep it a model of beauty and efficiency.

The Public Utilities Department's mission is to provide the Citizens of Mebane with clean, safe reliable water and to collect wastewater in a manner that protects public health and the environment. We strive to render the highest standard of customer service and maintain a "Positively Charming" role within the community.

Mebane NC 27302 Mebane Fire Department Founded in 1922, the Mebane Fire Department is an organization of dedicated professionals who are committed to serving the community by protecting life and property through prevention, education, emergency medical and fire services. Operating out of three stations, the Department covers a 35 square-mile district with a population of nearly 25,000. Each of the three stations are strategically located in areas that provide the best coverage and response to the citizens we serve. The Department consists of 53 members, including 17 volunteers and 36 career employees who cover the department 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. Those members operate a total of 13 fire apparatus, including 3 engine-pumpers, 2 engine-tankers, 2 brush trucks, 1 utility rescue truck, a 100-foot aerial platform, and 5 support vehicles. There is also a paramedic from Alamance County EMS that is housed at Station. The Department serves both a city and rural fire district and carries a Fire Suppression rating (formally known as ISO rating) of 2 inside the city and a 3 for the remaining rural district. This puts the Department as one of the highest rated combination paid/volunteer departments in the State. The Department also has an automatic mutual aid agreement with both Alamance and Orange Counties. The Mebane Fire Department is a registered Permanent Checking Station and a member of Alamance County Safe Kids and Buckle Up North Carolina . Parents and caregivers can receive information about child passenger safety (CPS) and have their child restraints and seat belts checked to be sure they are installed and used correctly. For more information click here . For more information, non-emergency calls, or to arrange a tour or event, please contact us at (919) 563-5718. For emergencies, dial 911.

The Mebane Police Department is committed to providing the best law enforcement services available for our citizens and visitors. Staffing our agency with highly qualified professionals, our agency ensures quality service. Our overall success, however, will be achieved by the community working hand-in-hand with our department to make this a safe and friendly city. Through continuous progression we will maintain a quality of life that is modeled by all cities.

The Mebane Police Department is very pro-active in Community Oriented Policing. Officers work with the community to address the citizens’ concerns. Any citizen wishing to form a Community Watch program in their neighborhood should contact the Police Department for more information.

Safety and Polices The City of Mebane is dedicated to the protection of its employees from on-the-job injuries and illnesses. The City’s Health and Safety Programs, including all sections and attachments, is intended to ensure that the City meets the requirements of 29 CFR 1926 and 1910, as well as regulations set forth by the Department of Transportation (DOT). The IT Help Desk is the City of Mebane's central point for technology support. It serves two audiences: residents who need help with city digital services, and city employees who need technical support to do their jobs.

If you are a resident or business owner having trouble with a City of Mebane online service — paying a bill, completing a form, viewing a meeting livestream, or using public Wi-Fi at a city facility — the Help Desk can usually point you in the right direction.

Email is the preferred way to reach us because it creates a tracked record we can refer back to and lets us include screenshots, links, or other details. Phone and online requests are also welcome — use whatever works best for you.
Trouble loading or using pages on the city website
Issues connecting to Mebane-Public WiFi at city facilities
Questions about the city's online tools, such as the Citizen Report Application and Parks Application
Online form: Submit a request at any time

Mebane city employees can report technology issues, request new equipment or software, and ask questions through the Help Desk during business hours. Email is the preferred channel for most requests because it creates a tracked record. Call for urgent or security-related issues.
Email is the preferred method for routine requests because it creates a tracked record with timestamps and a written history of the issue.
When you email a request, include the following in the body of the message:
What you were trying to do, what happened instead, and any error message exactly as it appeared
When the problem started and whether anything changed before it started
Whether the issue is affecting others in your department, or just you
Your direct phone extension so we can reach you if we need more information
A screenshot if relevant — pasted directly into the email is fine

Development Map Welcome to the interactive Mebane Development Map! Below you can explore projects in various phases of development including proposed rezoning projects with site plans and projects that are approved and/or under construction. Projects that require a rezoning or special use permit will have a case number associated with the public hearing process. Projects that do not require a rezoning are known as “by right” developments. These projects are reviewed and approved by the Mebane Technical Review Committee (TRC) before they are published on this interactive map resource. For any questions or more information about the projects below please contact the Planning and Zoning Department.
The City of Mebane wants residents to know how to protect themselves, what we will and will not do, and how to report scams when they happen.
If someone contacts you claiming to be from the City of Mebane and does any of the following, it is not us:
Demand immediate payment by text message or threaten to shut off your service within the hour
Ask for your Social Security number, full bank account number, or online banking password
Tell you there is a warrant for your arrest unless you pay a fee
Send a link by text and tell you to click it to “verify” your account
Ask you to keep the conversation secret from family members or your bank

The North Carolina Attorney General has warned residents about a wave of text messages claiming to be from the NC DMV, threatening license suspension or fines for unpaid tickets. The real NC DMV does not collect traffic tickets by text and will never threaten you this way. If you receive one, do not click the link. Report it as spam and delete it.

You receive a call or text saying your water, power, or internet will be shut off within 30 minutes unless you pay immediately. The caller may even know your name and address from public records. Real utility providers, including the City of Mebane, send multiple written notices before service interruption and never demand payment by gift card.

Scammers are increasingly using artificial intelligence to mimic the voices of family members in distress. A call comes from someone who sounds exactly like your grandchild, child, or spouse, claiming they are in jail, in the hospital, or stranded and need money immediately. If you receive a call like this, hang up and call the family member directly using the number you already have for them. Consider agreeing on a family “safe word” that only real family members would know.
Slow down. Scammers rely on urgency and panic. A real emergency from a real agency will still be there in five minutes after you verify it through an official phone number.
Never share login codes. No legitimate organization will ever ask you to read back a code that was texted to you. That code is what the scammer needs to take over your account.
Talk to family. The single most effective protection against scams targeting seniors is a quick phone call to a family member before sending money or sharing information.
Contact your bank or card issuer immediately to stop or reverse the transaction.
File a complaint with the FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center at ic3.gov . This is the federal database used to investigate and disrupt scam operations.
This information is provided as general guidance. If you believe you are an active target of fraud or have lost money, contact your bank and the Mebane Police Department right away.

Menu Planning Board Board of Adjustment Racial Equity Advisory Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Commission Recreation and Parks Advocacy Commission Back Planning Board Meetings Back 2021 Planning Board Meetings 2022 Planning Board Meetings 2023 Planning Board Meetings 2024 Planning Board Meetings 2025 Planning Board Meetings Back REAC Meetings Back Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Commission Meetings Reports & Work Plans Back Recreation and Parks Advocacy Meetings Planning Board About The City of Mebane Planning Board is an advisory body that makes recommendations to the City Council on planning matters such as long-range development plans, land use studies, rezoning requests and text amendments to the Unified Development Ordinance. The Planning Board meets at City Hall on the second Monday of each month at 6:30 p.m., as business warrants. The regular meeting schedule for 2026 is listed below. To be heard at a Planning Board meeting, a complete application with associated fees must be submitted to the Planning and Zoning Department at least three weeks prior to the meeting date. The Planning Board consists of nine members, six of whom are residents of the City of Mebane and appointed by the City Council, and three members (two from Alamance County and one from Orange County) are residents of that area which is within one mile outside of the corporate limits of the city and are appointed by their respective County Commissioners. All positions are for a term of four years. Vacancies occurring otherwise are for the unexpired term. When vacancies occur, applications will be accepted from those who live within the designated area . The Board of Adjustment is a seven member board, five of which are the acting City Council members who represent the City's Corporate limits. The remaining two positions are appointed by Alamance and Orange County Commissioners as representatives of the extraterritorial jurisdiction. Meetings are held in conjunction with the City Council meeting on the first Monday of the month, at 6:00 P.M. in the Council Chambers of the Mebane Municipal Building at 106 E. Washington Street. In the event that a City observed holiday falls on the first Monday the meeting will be held the following Monday. Members Member Representative Area Katie Burkholder Corporate Limits Montrena W. For this year's annual activity challenge, the Mebane BPAC have 15 locations marked in Mebane with 11 unique code words. You are invited to join the hunt to find them all and solve the phrase. Many of the locations are places where you can get active in our community. You must visit and submit photos at 7 of the locations to qualify for Mebane swag. Follow the City of Mebane on social media for hints: https://linktr.ee/cityofmebane ! About Since adoption of the Bicycle and Pedestrian Transportation Plan in 2015, the City of Mebane created the Mebane Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Commission (BPAC) to advise the City Council on how to most effectively serve the bicycle, pedestrian, and related needs of Mebane. The BPAC includes seven members who meet monthly and are primarily supported by the City’s Planning and Recreation & Parks Departments. All BPAC members live in Mebane’s planning jurisdiction, bringing passion for the community and advocating for pedestrian and cyclist safety and accessibility. The BPAC meets at 6:00 p.m. on the fourth Monday of each month. All meetings of the BPAC are open to the public. Contact Us The BPAC would love to hear your suggestions for improving biking and walking around Mebane! Please email us at BPAC@cityofmebane.com . Menu Planning Board Board of Adjustment Racial Equity Advisory Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Commission Recreation and Parks Advocacy Commission Back Planning Board Meetings Back 2021 Planning Board Meetings 2022 Planning Board Meetings 2023 Planning Board Meetings 2024 Planning Board Meetings 2025 Planning Board Meetings Back REAC Meetings Back Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Commission Meetings Reports & Work Plans Back Recreation and Parks Advocacy Meetings Recreation and Parks Advocacy Commission About The Recreation and Parks Advocacy Commission provides direction to the City Council and the Recreation and Parks Department regarding matters related to Recreation and Parks programs, facilities, policies, and its long-range plan.  They serve as “Champions” of Recreation and Parks by promoting parks, programs, and events to other citizens, legislators, and others to understand, first hand, the essential value that Recreation and Parks has to our positively charming community. Their responsibilities include: Serve as a liaison between City officials and the citizens on Recreation and Parks matters; Provide guidance and feedback to the Recreation and Parks Department and City Council in matters affecting programs, facilities, policies, and long range plans for Recreation and Parks; Inform and educate the general public about the importance and need for Recreation and Parks programs, facilities and services; Volunteer to work with leaders in Recreation and Parks facilities, programs and activities; Assist in developing an updated master plan to meet the present and future needs for programs, services, park facilities, open spaces and to advise in establishing priorities for each of these; Recommend changes, updates, and the approval of rules, policy and procedures pertaining to the use of public parks and facilities, including fees and charges. The Advocacy Commission meets every other month on the 3rd Monday of those months at 6 pm, for approximately 2 hours, at the Mebane Arts and Community Center .  Special meetings may be called at any time as needed. The RPAC also has a Sub Committee called SPICE, that focuses on Senior Programming and other endeavors.  This Sub Committee meets at the MACC monthly during regular business hours. Bids may be sent electronically via email or by paper sent directly to the City of Mebane at:

Consideration will be given only to bids of Contractors who submit evidence that they are licensed under “An Act to Regulate the Practice of General Contracting”, ratified by the General Assembly of the State of North Carolina on March 10, 1925, and as subsequently amended, when such Act is applicable. Each bid shall be accompanied with a certified check drawn on a bank or trust company insured by the FDIC, or a Bid Bond prepared on the form of Bid Bond contained in the Bidding Documents or a Surety Company’s standard form and properly executed by a corporate surety licensed under the laws of North Carolina to execute such bonds. Menu Lead Service Line Inventory City of Mebane Standards and Specifiations Annual Drinking Water Quality Reports Fire Flow Testing and Modeling Cross Connection Fats, Oils, & Greases (FOG) Water Shortage Response Plan PFAS/1,4-dioxane Information FAQ Contact Public Utilities Back FOG – Additional Resources Kyle Smith Public Utilities Director (919) 563-3401 Contact us Physical Address 636 Corregidor Street Mebane, NC 27302 Mailing Address 106 E Washington Street Mebane, NC 27302 Monday through Friday 8:00 am - 5:00 pm After Hours Emergency (919) 563-9031 Lead Service Line Inventory What is a Service Line? In a water distribution system, a service line carries water from the water main (typically near or under the street) to your place of residence or business. The EPA classifies water service lines as any water line that is less than two (2”) inches in diameter. The City of Mebane owns and maintains the water service up to the meter, and the property owner owns and maintains the water service from the meter to the building. Provided below is a diagram of a typical water service line with the public and private ownership side labeled. To view the water service line material to your dwelling, please click the link provided below. If you have any questions about your service line or have not already submitted your customer self-identification form, please send an email to LCRI@cityofmebane.com . If you have lost your customer ID form, or if you do not see your material listed, please submit your water service material type to LCRI@cityofmebane.com LSL Inventory Background about Lead Service Lines Throughout the history of water utilities, Lead Service Lines were sometimes used as a water service material throughout the United States as a way to convey water from the main to a residential home or business. Due to a better understanding of Lead and the harmful long-term effects, many water projects limited the amount of lead that was used in construction of water mains and services starting around the 1930’s and 40’s. While lead was not used for the entirety of the service lines, there were lead fittings that were being used until 1986. In 1986, the US government passed a lead ban that stated that water piping or fittings that were to be used for distribution of water for consumption could not be lead. In 1992, the Safe Drinking Water Act was amended with an effective date of 1994 so that piping could not be manufactured with more than 8% lead and fixtures, solder or flux could not contain more than 0.2%. In 2011, congress passed the Reduction of Lead in Drinking Water Act (RLDWA) changing the definition of lead free from the 8% to a weighted average of 0.25% for all piping and fixtures while solder and flux remained at 0.2%.  In 2020, the EPA published the final “lead free” rule which requires manufacturers to constantly verify that materials are adhering to the 0.25% and 0.2%  standards. This updated rule also requires public water supply owners to create an inventory of service line materials to further identify and address potential sources of lead contamination within drinking water. Requirements of the City of Mebane As part of the EPA’s rule adoption in 2020, all public water suppliers are required to inventory the water service lines for residential single family, multifamily homes, daycares and schools. In an effort to comply with these rules, the City of Mebane has begun investigating water service material types in various locations around the City. The locations that were selected to be investigated first are areas of older homes and areas that have a higher probability of having a lead service line. The City of Mebane was awarded a grant to begin the field investigations and has contracted with a local engineering consultant to lead the investigating efforts. In the coming weeks you may notice a contractor digging around the water service to identify the service material type. The contractor will be verifying the material of the service no less than eighteen (but no greater than twenty-four) inches away from the meter to comply with the EPA’s requirements. The contractor will be responsible for filling all holes in around the meter and sowing grass seed and straw on top of disturbed areas. At the completion of the investigation, the City of Mebane will publish the results for residents to view. The City of Mebane will comply with the EPA regulations that requires all lead service lines to be replaced within ten years of the publishing of the service line inventory. While the City cannot investigate every service at this moment, we do ask for your help. If you know the material of your water service, please send an email to the address below with your Name, Address, and Water Service Material. If you have recently replaced the water service at your home, please include the year the replacement was made. LCRI@cityofmebane.com We thank you for your help as we strive to keep the City of Mebane Positively Charming.

VIDEOS FROM ORANGE COUNTY, NORTH CAROLINA Stay up to date with your County by checking out our YouTube Channel! FEATURED VIDEOS Volunteer Spotlight: Jackie Podger Volunteers make Orange County the best it can be. One way residents can make a positive impact on Orange County is by volunteering to serve on a board or commission. In this video , Jackie Podger discusses her volunteer work on the Adult Care Home and Nursing Home Community Advisory Committee. Welcome to Breeze Farm! Breeze Farm is a 270-acre research and education farm located just north of Hillsborough, North Carolina. It is a farm incubator where aspiring and new farmers can rent land and use the facilities to improve their skills and build a customer base. To help protect farmland and farming as a way of life in rural communities consider becoming a friend of Breeze Farm . Need help understanding your tax bill? Property owners in Orange County, NC, began receiving their property tax bills on July 31. The bill contains the property taxes due to each taxing jurisdiction based on the location of the property. This video explains your tax bill in plain language. Get a detailed explanation of your tax bill at www.orangecountync.gov/TaxBill FOLLOW US ON SOCIAL MEDIA

Website Sign In Home Government Departments G - Z Solid Waste Management Educational Resources A-Z Waste & Recycle Guide A-Z Waste and Recycling Guide Purpose This directory is to help users properly dispose of, or divert items from, landfill disposal. Orange County Solid Waste Management does not promote any particular business. The mention of any business or organization does not constitute its endorsement by the County. This is only to serve as a guide and is not necessarily complete. However, if you would like an item added let us know by emailing recycling . Reuse Reuse is better than recycling! For used but useful, operational, and clean items consider offering them for reuse at the Salvage Sheds located at Waste and Recycling Centers (except Bradshaw Quarry). For a list of items accepted at the Salvage Sheds, please review this page . All items left at the Salvage Sheds must be clean and in working condition. There are also many second hand stores throughout our community that accept clean items in good working order.  You can find a list of local organizations here .  If you know of an organization or individual who would accept items for reuse, repair or recycling that belong on this list, let us know by emailing recycling .

Mission DEAPR-Recreation Division exists to provide Orange County residents of all ages, backgrounds, abilities, and interests with a wide variety of affordable recreation programming, open access to recreation facilities, and opportunities for all people to gather, celebrate, and engage in activities that promote healthy lifestyles, teach important life skills, and build a stronger community. Orange County Recreation offers over 100 programs per year intended to engage and enrich both the individual and the community. The program guides above provide information regarding current and up-coming programs. The following links allow you to browse the programs we offer year round for what might be of interest to you and your family now and in the future.

The Community Relations Department is responsible for public information and community engagement. It works to strengthen communication with residents and businesses by promoting active engagement and sharing information through online resources, brochures, social media, and traditional news outlets. Our Services This Week in Orange County Newsletter is a weekly email that explores the programs and activities of Orange County, NC Government departments. Orange County Government Academy is a comprehensive, eight-week program designed to educate residents about the inner workings of Orange County government. View the curriculum and schedule of the Orange County Government Academy. Guide to Orange County Government is a printed booklet that helps residents and visitors to learn about the many programs and services provided by Orange County Government. Orange County Roadshows are free, family-friendly events that let community members access government services in an informal setting near home. Orange County Government Gazette is published on the first Wednesday of each month in The News of Orange and highlights programs and services from your county government. Manage www.orangecountync.gov The Community Giving Fund supports Orange County departments and programs through donations. All gifts are tax-deductible and can include cash, stocks, bonds, or property, and the money is used to support the county’s budget. Strategic Communications Plan helps people get more involved by improving communication, using inclusive ways to connect, and strengthening community relationships. The Community Giving Fund was established in January 2013 and is intended to be an encompassing fund for Orange County government. Donations can be given to many departments or county-sponsored programs through this fund, which is managed by Orange County and Truist. The dollars are used to supplement the county's operating budget.
Contributions can be made online via our portal , which is hosted by Truist Bank.
Donations can also be made by sending a check to:
Please designate Community Giving Fund and the department or program you would like your contribution to fund on your check.

Website Sign In Home Government Departments A - F Community Relations Community Giving Fund Tropical Storm Chantal Donations Tropical Storm Chantal Donations Help Orange County, Carrboro, Chapel Hill, and Hillsborough Recover Tropical Storm Chantal left a significant mark on our community. The Tropical Storm Chantal community giving fund is dedicated to supporting local recovery efforts across Orange County and the towns of Carrboro, Chapel Hill, and Hillsborough. This fund is a collaborative effort between the county and the towns to ensure donated funds are prioritized for critical needs and equitably distributed across the county. Your contribution will provide essential resources and services in the following areas: General Recovery Fund Your donation will be allocated where it’s most needed across all five recovery areas. If you want to donate to a specific area outside of the five listed below, place it in the "description" box during the payment process. Economic Provide support to the recovery of small businesses Drive local economic growth Strengthen and support the local workforce Housing Assist displaced survivors with safe and temporary housing Rehabilitate, improve safety, and preserve housing for homeowners Ensure continuation of and streamlined access to existing housing support programs, including emergency housing assistance Health and Human Services Promote access to mental and behavioral health services, including support for impacted children Provide direct individual assistance to survivors (clothing, food, medications, and other personal items) Provide support for medical services including replacement of medication, assistive devices, and support for residents with disabilities Natural and Cultural Resources Preserve and restore natural resources Provide support to cultural, recreational, and arts programs Infrastructure Preserve and restore critical community infrastructure such as municipal water and wastewater systems Support with debris and waste management services Orange County Arts Commission The flood-damaged Eno Arts Mill, the home of the Orange County Arts Commission, needs financial support to rebuild: artsorange.org/give Donations by Mail If you prefer to donate via mail, please write the check to Orange County Government . Mail to: Orange County Finance PO Box 8181 Hillsborough, NC 27278 ATTN: Accounting

Orange County Animal Services Animal Services helps pets and people in the community, and focuses on healthy and positive ways to reduce pet overpopulation. You can adopt a pet, volunteer to help shelter pets, and donate to support pets in need. Our Services Adopt your new best friend from our shelter in Chapel Hill, North Carolina. Orange County Animal Control ensures that both people and pets remain safe, reducing conflicts and promoting a healthy, compassionate community. Bringing Animals to the Shelter Get Assistance Spay/Neuter Resources Lost & Found Pets Free-Roaming Cats Low-Cost Vaccine Clinics Microchip Scanning Stations Training Resources Pet Food Assistance Housing Resources Community Animal Clinic Wildlife Resources Animal Services Advisory Board Public Information Request Get Involved Dog Day Out Foster Opportunities Volunteer Opportunities Donate Become a Rescue Partner Calendar of events Follow Us! Sign up for our newsletter Facebook Instagram YouTube About Us The Animal Services Department strives to meet the needs of residents by working with volunteers and other partners to deliver cost-effective and integrated sheltering and animal control services that ensure the health, safety and well being of humans, pets and the community as a whole. Addressing the problem of pet overpopulation with a proactive and positive approach is an integral ingredient of the department’s mission. Review our achievements . Contact Us Orange County Animal Services is located at 1601 Eubanks Road, Chapel Hill, NC 27516 Call 919-942-7387 or email our team for more information. View operating hours . More information about the team at Animal Services and other detailed contact information . If you need information in another format or language, or need an accommodation, please contact us.

Home Government Departments A - F Animal Services Pet Help Resources Spay /  Neuter Spay /  Neuter Orange County Animal Services (OCAS) wants you to know about spay/neuter options to help control pet overpopulation by reducing unwanted litters. Spaying and neutering pets means fewer pets end up in shelters. Spaying and neutering saves lives and ultimately creates a healthier community for pets and people. No-Cost And Low-Cost Spaying and Neutering If you are a DSS client or qualify by your income, you can receive a spay/neuter voucher to be used at one of our specific partners for your pet. If you do not qualify for no-cost services, we can let you know about low-cost options. Orange County North Carolina residents: fill out this Inquiry Form and someone will contact you regarding spay/neuter assistance for your pets. ( See Inquiry Form in Spanish here .)* *Due to the high volume of inquiries received, it may take up to 2 months to issue vouchers to those that qualify for the program. Thank you for your patience! Managing Pet Overpopulation Orange County Animal Services and other members of the community work together to manage pet overpopulation in Orange County. The goal is to reduce the number of animals that enter the shelter; control and manage the costs of animal services; and drastically reduce the use of euthanasia as a means of population control. View statistics about spay and neuter in Orange County North Carolina. Managing Free-Roaming Cats in Orange County View our web page with more information about free-roaming cats. These cats are sometimes called working cats or community cats. We have implemented a spay/neuter program to help prevent unwanted litters and reduce the free-roaming cat population in our community. Simply removing these cats from an area can be an invitation for more cats to enter that territory, and this older method has proven not to be effective. So, people are frequently caught in a vicious cycle where free-roaming cat populations are not controlled. Spaying/neutering is a humane and proactive approach to free-roaming/community cat issues. Our community cat spay/neuter program is NOT for pets like socialized house cats or pet cats that live indoors only part time. This program targets community cats (free-roaming, outdoor cats) that are typically not social (often feral or semi-feral) and live in small groups or in colonies on an Orange County property. They are fearful and not animals that would normally be considered adoptable. This process will involve a community cat caretaker that can ideally assist with trapping of the cats. A refundable deposit will be required for traps ($50, cash or check only). If you have free-roaming cats (sometimes called working cats or community cats) on your Orange County property that need to be spayed or neutered, you may be able to have them spayed or neutered FOR FREE. Submit inquiries about free-roaming cat spay/neuter assistance. Donate to help with this program and other lifesaving initiatives at OCAS.

Website Sign In Home Government Departments G - Z Health Department FIT Program of Orange County FIT Program of Orange County This program helps people who are getting out of prison and have a chronic health condition. (FIT stands for Formerly Incarcerated Transition). How FIT helps people getting out of prison We help them make a re-entry plan. We get them medical care at Piedmont Health Services. We help them get and manage their medicines. We provide referrals to treatment for: Mental health Substance abuse Nutrition Stopping to smoke, vape, or chew What is a chronic illness? A chronic illness is something that may not go away but with help, it can be managed. Here are some examples of chronic illnesses: Diabetes High blood pressure Heart disease Lung disease Kidney failure Liver disease Mental illness Substance abuse How long can someone get help from the FIT Program? Clients can receive services for up to two years after release. Contact Tommy Green Community Health Worker, FIT Program (919) 245-2397 hgreen@orangecountync.gov

The Family Success Alliance helps families get the support they need to thrive. We partner with local organizations to help families find education, health care, and housing.

We work with Orange County families who have children from birth through age 18 and who qualify for Free and Reduced Lunch. Family Navigators visit families in their homes to learn about their strengths and challenges. Based on each family’s goals and needs, navigators help them find support and build connections. Families can stay in the program for as long as they need.
Family Reading Partners — Provides books and parent education to encourage reading with young children
Hope Renovations — Trades training for women and home repairs for older adults

The goal of Orange County's Eviction Diversion Program is to help tenants avoid eviction, and when possible, to keep you in your home. The program also exists to help resolve issues between you and your landlord.
They must go through the court process to make that happen. We can offer you free legal advice about your housing issues and possible representation in court. To apply for help:

Step 2 : The Housing Helpline will return your phone call or email during business hours. Make sure your phone is set up to receive voicemails, and your voicemail is not full. Housing Helpline staff will confirm your information and refer your case to the Eviction Diversion Program if you need legal assistance.

The EHA program assists households in Orange County that are earning 30% or less of Area Median Income (AMI) and have experienced a Life-Changing Event in the last three months which affects their ability to pay rent. Life-Changing Events include the death, illness or loss of employment of a household member who was contributing toward rent. Orange County residents with active court summons for eviction are also eligible.
We may be able to help with some or all of the following: Past due rent Late pet and other fees to landlords, and Housing-related court fees
Late pet and other fees to landlords, and
Applications without all the necessary documentation will be denied. Please do not submit an application until you have all the necessary documentation.

We process applications based on the date received. There is a cap of $4,500 in assistance, and due to limited funds we are only able to assist 12 households per month. We are unable to give status updates on how long until your household will be assisted. Households are limited to one EHA award per fiscal year ( July to June )
If you have not received a court summons or had a Life-Changing Event in the last three months, you do not qualify for EHA from Orange County.
Please contact the Housing Helpline for information about other community resources that may be able to help you with your housing needs.

Use our Submittable online portal to apply Include a copy of the court summons, your updated lease, a current ledger, and proof of income. You can get a copy of the lease and ledger from your landlord.
We process your application We work on applications with the earliest court dates. We will reach out if we need more information from you or your landlord. Call us if your eviction status changes.
We work on applications with the earliest court dates. We will reach out if we need more information from you or your landlord. Call us if your eviction status changes.
We approve or deny your request We may be able to approve an application if your landlord will accept a payment from us, and you have not met the funding cap of ($4,500)
We reach out to your landlord If we approve your request, we send papers to your landlord to be sure they will accept EHA, and to protect you from being evicted during this time.
If we approve your request, we send papers to your landlord to be sure they will accept EHA, and to protect you from being evicted during this time.
County Finance sends the payment It may be up to two months between the time you submit your application and the county pays your Landlord.

The Single Family Rehabilitation (SFR) program provides larger, more comprehensive repairs to homes for eligible homeowners to extend the usable life of their homes. The program is funded by the North Carolina Housing Finance Agency's Essential Single Family Rehabilitation Loan Pool and local County funds.

Website Sign In Home Government Departments G - Z Social Services About DSS Volunteer & Donor Opportunities Holiday Program Donate Toys Donate Toys The Toy Chest attempts to provide new toys to each eligible child in Orange County. The number of eligible children continues to increase each year and the Department of Social Services relies on individuals, businesses, community groups, schools and churches to meet all these needs. Benefits of Donating Donating to the Toy Chest is a great way for young children, Girl Scout and Boy Scout Troops, classrooms and Sunday Schools to get involved. Through toy drives, toy donation box collection sites and “bring-a-gift” parties they can help children their age have a joyful holiday. Toy drives are also a great way for businesses to increase employee morale and give back to the community. Please consider donating toys to the Toy Chest so that each child in Orange County will have at least one new toy on Christmas morning. How to Donate Toys Step 1 Collect or purchase new, unopened toys or gift cards. Not sure what to get? Here is a list of Gift Ideas (PDF) . Step 2 Bring toys to either office location of the Department of Social Services during regular business hours by December 9th. Please deliver any gift cards directly to staff, or drop off toys at one of these Toys for Tots drop-off locations . Step 3 Be sure to leave your name and address for a receipt. Remember, all donations are tax deductible. Or you can donate directly by clicking on the Donate button below.

Click to jump to: Fit Feet Fit Seniors Fit Seniors Program Waiver Live Stream Disclaimer Health and Wellness is a program of the Department on Aging that offers an array of services, ranging from massages to free blood pressure screenings. We also offer many fitness classes like yoga, aerobics, tai chi, and much more. C heck out the current Endless Possibilities Activity Guide to find out all that we offer, including wellness special events, exercise classes, individual services, and support groups.

You might be having a difficult time taking care of your feet and trimming your toenails. If you are not as mobile as you used to be, or you have poor eyesight it can make it harder to take care of your feet. The Fit Feet program can help keep your feet in better condition with a foot health assessment and nail trimming service.
Examine the feet for obvious or potential problems
Referral to the client’s medical provider if obvious or potential foot health problems are found
If no problems are found, an RN will trim and file the client's toenails

What should you do to take care of your feet or nails while the Fit Feet Clinic is closed? What constitutes a foot care emergency? If you are new to the Fit Feet program what can you do while you wait to be seen? These are just a few of the questions that people might be asking themselves. This information is a brief attempt to address these concerns.

There are several things you can do to take care of your feet while waiting for your next appointment. This might include applying lotion or using anti-fungal medication. Daily application of lotion helps to limit callous formation. If you have toenail fungus it is important to apply topical medication daily as directed. Your home care is essential to successful treatment.

Conditions such as diabetes can cause loss of sensation, i.e. peripheral neuropathy, so that you might cut yourself if you if you tried to cut your own nails. For this reason, we recommend using a nail file to smooth the rough edges of the nails on a weekly basis.

If you are waiting to sign up for the Fit Feet Program there are some things you can do in advance. You can find information about the program on the website. You will need to get written permission from your doctor to be seen at the Fit Feet Clinic. You can download a permission sheet to send to your doctor and ask him to fax it back to the Senior Center. This will expedite setting up the initial appointment once the clinic reopens.

Fit Seniors Program Waiver The Fit Seniors program requires physical activity that may present problems if certain medical conditions currently exist. It is our recommendation that the participants consult their physician if they have any questions or concerns about participation in this program. If you choose to participate in this exercise video you agree to hold harmless any persons or organizations involved with the Wellness program exercise videos from any legal action or claims at any time due to your participation in this video. Be sure that you are in good enough physical condition to participate safely.

Website Sign In Home Government Departments A - F Aging Medicare Assistance Medicare Assistance The Seniors’ Health Insurance Information Program (SHIIP) provides free, unbiased education and assistance with Medicare. We can help you understand when and how to enroll, how to choose between the different Medicare options, and how to read your Medicare statements. We offer classes and one-on-one counseling appointments. You can meet with a certified counselor by appointment at the Seymour Center in Chapel Hill, the Passmore Center in Hillsborough or via Zoom Video Chat.  Make an appointment by clicking the Book Now Button below or calling us at 919-245-4274. You can also contact the North Carolina Department of Insurance for immediate assistance with all types of Medicare issues over the telephone. You can reach them 8 am - 5 pm Monday through Friday at 1-855-408-1212. Website Sign In Home Government Departments A - F Aging Tax Return Assistance Tax Return Assistance V olunteer I ncome T ax A ssistance (VITA) VITA is an Orange County Volunteer Connect 55+ (VC 55+) volunteer program that provides FREE federal and NC state income tax preparation to eligible individuals with low-to-moderate income, regardless of age or county of residence. The Orange County VITA tax filing season has ended. We will open our appointment lines and online booking for next year around January 15, 2027. Watch your mailbox in early January for any notices from the IRS (including annual IP PIN), your workplace and savings accounts and save these notices for your tax appointment. Actions taxpayers should take if they missed April filing and payment deadline Taxpayers who missed the April 15 filing deadline should submit their federal tax return as soon as possible. Those who missed the deadline to file and owe taxes should file as soon as possible to avoid penalties and interest. Requesting an extension allows for additional time to file but not to pay taxes owed. Interest and penalties will continue to accrue on the owed taxes until the balance is paid in full. Taxpayers who still need to file can use electronic filing options such as IRS Free File , or VITA volunteer-assisted GetYourRefund by Code for America. These are available through Oct. 15, 2026. Where’s My Tax Refund? If your return was electronically filed and you provided bank account information for a direct deposit, you will usually receive your federal refund within 21 days. You can check on the status of your refund at www.irs.gov/refunds or by downloading the IRS2Go app . If you filed your return on paper or did not provide bank information, you may receive a CP53E notice in the mail from the IRS requesting that you provide your bank information. If you don’t respond to the notice, the IRS will issue a paper check after 6 weeks. For updates on your refund status, visit Where’s My Refund . For NC state income tax refunds, allow six weeks for your refund to be processed and visit www.ncdor.gov/refund for more information. Questions? Contact Laura Prinzhorn at 919-245-4240. Click to jump to: Public Transit Transportation Resources Volunteer Driver Program Classes and Trips Get help finding alternative transportation options in Orange County by contacting the Department on Aging Transportation Specialist, Brandi Beeker, at 919-717-1853 or email Brandi Beeker . The Transportation Specialist is responsible for building awareness of current transportation services and helping to improve these services for the older adult population. Although the Transportation Specialist does not provide direct transportation, she is available to help identify transportation options for non-drivers and help with accessing services. The Transportation Specialist advocates for, and works to support, the transportation needs of the older adult community in Orange County.

Volunteer Connect 55+ (VC 55+) is a program of the Orange County Department on Aging to enrich the lives of people 55+ in Orange County by connecting them to meaningful volunteer experiences in the community. VC 55+ offers a new way for older adults to fulfill their passions and interests through diverse volunteer opportunities with other older adults.
The campaign leads up to World Elder Abuse Awareness Day on Monday, June 15, when community members are encouraged to wear purple to show support for older adults and commitment to preventing abuse.

An outreach table will be hosted at the Department on Aging on June 12 from 1 to 2 p.m., at 103 Meadowlands Drive in Hillsborough (connected to the SportsPlex) where residents can pick up information packets and resources
Purple pinwheels will be displayed at county departments and partner locations as a visual symbol of awareness
Yard signs will be placed throughout the community to promote the campaign
Orange County Clerk of Court Mark Kleinschmidt will speak on June 8 at 3 p.m. on the Out on the Edge radio show to discuss elder abuse prevention
Check in on older and/or disabled neighbors and loved ones
You do not have to prove abuse is happening to make a report. Speaking up may help save someone from harm.

Home News Flash Orange County Department on Aging Director Janice Tyler Honored with Pioneer Award
Orange County Department on Aging Director Janice Tyler Honored with Pioneer Award

The North Carolina Coalition on Aging created the Pioneer Award to honor individuals who have made exceptional, long-term contributions to aging services in the state. The award also helps raise awareness about the ongoing work to meet the needs of older North Carolinians.

Under Janice’s leadership, the Department on Aging and its two senior centers have become models for comprehensive senior services. Both centers are recognized as Centers of Excellence by the NC Division of Aging and are nationally accredited by the National Council on Aging .
Launching Project EngAGE , a 13-week training program that prepares older adults to serve as community resource leaders
Forming the Aging Transitions Team , which collaborates with social workers, therapists, and EMS to support older adults and their families
Expanding services for diverse and bilingual populations
Chair of the NC Senior Center Alliance

Website Sign In Home Government Departments A - F Aging Master Aging Plan SHARP Direct Care Worker Awards SHARP Direct Care Worker Awards The Orange County Department on Aging has partnered with the community group, Senior Health Advocacy and Resource Partners of Orange County (SHARP), to recognize Direct Care Workers serving clients in Orange County, NC. Direct care workers impact their client’s lives on a daily basis and make a difference in our community. Each year six awards will be presented one in each of five different categories and one person will be chosen as "Direct Care Worker of the Year." Visit the links to the left to see previous award winners. Scroll down to view the 2025 SHARP Awards Nomination Form. Categories & Special Criteria Awards will be given in five categories with special attention to the criteria as noted: Client Impact - Frequently recognized or praised for their efforts that make an impact on the quality of their clients lives. Going the Extra Mile - Passionate about providing extraordinary care to their clients in ways that exceed expectations. Leadership - Serves as a role model and demonstrates teamwork and willingness to help other direct care workers and staff. Longevity - Has performed work as a caregiver each year for the last 10 or more years and plans to continue working in the field. Rising Star - Has been a caregiver for less than three years and demonstrates enormous promise in the profession. If selected as the Award Recipient the individual agrees to be represented in photographs and videos, as well as included in any and all public relations ads and media campaigns. Eligibility To be eligible, direct care workers must have served clients in Orange County within the past year. Each person may be nominated in two categories. Each nominee must: Provide direct care to clients as a primary function of their job. Have at least six consecutive months of service in the past year. Maintain and project a professional demeanor and exhibit dependability. Actively demonstrate pride in being a direct care worker and selflessly serve others in the course of their duties. Selection Process The SHARP Award winners will be selected by representatives from Project EngAGE. Project EngAGE is a senior leadership program sponsored by Orange County Department on Aging. The SHARP Committee is comprised of professionals from senior care, healthcare and caregiving support organizations from the Greater Orange County community. Nomination Form The deadline to submit nominations is October 10, 2025. To submit the Direct Care Worker Nomination Form online, please click here . To download the Direct Care Worker Nomination Form, please click here . Please email completed nomination forms to kmcmillan@orangecountync.gov or return in-person at 2551 Homestead Road, Chapel Hill, NC 27516. Awards Ceremony A recognition ceremony will be held for award winners on Thursday, November 13 at the Seymour Center in Chapel Hill. For more information about the Direct Care Worker Awards, email Kendall McMillan or call (919) 245-4278. The Orange County Department on Aging has partnered with the community group, Senior Health Advocacy and Resource Partners of Orange County (SHARP), to recognize Direct Care Workers serving clients in Orange County, NC. Direct care workers impact their client’s lives on a daily basis and make a difference in our community. Each year six awards will be presented one in each of five different categories and one person will be chosen as "Direct Care Worker of the Year." Visit the links to the left to see previous award winners.
Scroll down to view the 2025 SHARP Awards Nomination Form.
Awards will be given in five categories with special attention to the criteria as noted:
Client Impact - Frequently recognized or praised for their efforts that make an impact on the quality of their clients lives.
Going the Extra Mile - Passionate about providing extraordinary care to their clients in ways that exceed expectations.
Leadership - Serves as a role model and demonstrates teamwork and willingness to help other direct care workers and staff.
Longevity - Has performed work as a caregiver each year for the last 10 or more years and plans to continue working in the field.
Rising Star - Has been a caregiver for less than three years and demonstrates enormous promise in the profession.
If selected as the Award Recipient the individual agrees to be represented in photographs and videos, as well as included in any and all public relations ads and media campaigns.
To be eligible, direct care workers must have served clients in Orange County within the past year. Each person may be nominated in two categories.
Provide direct care to clients as a primary function of their job.
Have at least six consecutive months of service in the past year.
Maintain and project a professional demeanor and exhibit dependability.
Actively demonstrate pride in being a direct care worker and selflessly serve others in the course of their duties.
The SHARP Award winners will be selected by representatives from Project EngAGE. Project EngAGE is a senior leadership program sponsored by Orange County Department on Aging.
The SHARP Committee is comprised of professionals from senior care, healthcare and caregiving support organizations from the Greater Orange County community.
A recognition ceremony will be held for award winners on Thursday, November 13 at the Seymour Center in Chapel Hill.

Chapel Hill Transit is a fare-free system. Just hop on and ride! The easiest way to track our buses in real time, receive service alerts, and plan your trip across local and regional routes is with the Transit App. We also offer a downloadable system and route maps.
These are locations where you can switch to other routes or regional buses.
Numbered symbols represent bus stops on the map.
Find the stop closest to your starting point.
Time points are columns of times under stop names on the timetable.
They show when the bus is scheduled to leave that stops.
Timepoints are also on the route map.

Our Solid Waste Services Division provides weekly household trash and yard trimmings collection as well as numerous other seasonal services and paid services. Learn more about our services, including how to pay for the paid services and other Frequently Asked Questions.
Commercial trash will be collected earlier in the week.

We offer income-based fee assistance for our paid Solid Waste services, including roll cart purchases, bulky item pickup, and large brush pile collection. Anyone who qualifies and has already purchased a roll cart or other service is eligible for a retroactive partial refund dating back to July 1, 2025. See the Frequently Asked Questions below for more details.
Using our online payment portal is a quick and easy way to purchase roll carts or pay for services such as bulky item pickup and large brush pile pickup.
We collect Christmas trees at no charge.
Call Public Works at 919-969-5100 during business hours to make a purchase over the phone using a credit card.
Search for your address in the Solid Waste Service Area Eligibility Check .
These services are already paid for by property taxes:
You do not need to set up a special account for those services.
Trash or yard trimmings that aren't out by 6:00am on your collection day will be picked up the next week.
The University provides trash collection on campus.
When you move, leave the cart at the residence as they are the property of the Town.
Our office is open from 8am to 5pm on weekdays. Turnaround time to process payment may take up to two business days.
Make sure to put your trash cart behind the curb by 6am on your trash day.
Remember to take your cart from the curb by 7pm on the collection day to not get a fine .

Put your trash bags inside your trash cart. Trash bags placed outside the trash cart won't be collected. Town ordinance requires that our crews not collect household trash that's not in bags and/or outside a trash can.
Town ordinance requires that our crews not collect household trash that's not in bags and/or outside a trash can.
If you have large items and electronics to throw away, you must pay for them to be picked up by the town.
If you normally use a rear alley for trash collections, please put your items in front of your house.
If you have a trash dumpster that the town empties, put your items near the dumpster. Please make sure not to put your items behind, directly in front of, or leaning on the dumpster.
We normally collect bulky items and electronics within five business days of payment. When we have a lot of requests, it may take longer than five business days for your items to be picked up.
Items can also be taken to one of Orange County's Waste and Recycling Centers for free
Our office is open from 8am to 5pm on weekdays
We pick up yard trimmings once per week at homes that have the town's curbside trash collections. Property taxes pay for this service, so you don't need to set up a special account.
We take the yard trimmings to Orange County's Disposal Center. The county chips the yard trimmings into mulch and offers it for sale.
The replacement cost of yard trimming carts is the responsibility of the resident. All carts are delivered directly to your address.
All carts are delivered directly to your address.
The Town encourages home composting of yard trimmings, and information about composting is available online.
Keep any loose piles of limbs and branches under three cubic yards. This is about the size of a small pickup truckload. Put loose yard trimmings into one pile. Don't have more than one pile.
For options for piles that are bigger than three cubic yards, see the Large Yard Trimmings Collection section.
All branches and limbs must be less than four feet long and ten inches in diameter.
We pick up real Christmas trees on yard trimming collection days.
Fake trees that won't fit inside a trash bag in your trash cart, can be picked up as a bulky item.
You can recycle Christmas lights at any Orange County Waste and Recycling Center .

What about Large Brush piles? Is your yard trimming pile bigger than three cubic yards, which is about the size of a pickup truckload? If you're not sure, use the following guide. Remember to have all your yard trimmings in just one pile. For large piles that are bigger than three cubic yards Knuckleboom Truck Collection This is a large truck with a claw-like arm that scoops up piles from the side of the street. The cost is $125 per truckload. Orange County Solid Waste Center Residents can take one truckload of yard trimmings per day or weekend to an Orange County Waste and Recycling Center for free. Yard materials we don't pick up The town doesn't pick up loose leaves, large logs, stumps, crossties, or clearing debris from lots. Contractors or builders must remove these items.

Is your yard trimming pile bigger than three cubic yards, which is about the size of a pickup truckload? If you're not sure, use the following guide. Remember to have all your yard trimmings in just one pile. For large piles that are bigger than three cubic yards Knuckleboom Truck Collection This is a large truck with a claw-like arm that scoops up piles from the side of the street. The cost is $125 per truckload. Orange County Solid Waste Center Residents can take one truckload of yard trimmings per day or weekend to an Orange County Waste and Recycling Center for free. Yard materials we don't pick up The town doesn't pick up loose leaves, large logs, stumps, crossties, or clearing debris from lots. Contractors or builders must remove these items.
Knuckleboom Truck Collection This is a large truck with a claw-like arm that scoops up piles from the side of the street. The cost is $125 per truckload.
Orange County Solid Waste Center Residents can take one truckload of yard trimmings per day or weekend to an Orange County Waste and Recycling Center for free.
The University handles their own recycling services on campus.
The Town picks up dead animals from the side of the roads that the Town maintains. The Town won't pick up dead animals from private property .
Click here for fees and more information.
Put your trash bags inside your trash dumpster. Never put them on the ground outside the dumpster. Trash bags outside the dumpster won't be collected and can cause the dumpster to not be emptied.
Each business in the downtown compactor district pays fees to use the compactor. The fees are based on the amount of waste the business makes.

The Town of Chapel Hill only accepts online applications through governmentjobs.com. All job offers are conditional, pending the results of a background check for criminal and driving records and the results of a drug test.
See the results of our employee pay and classification study.

Health insurance is administered by Blue Cross Blue Shield of North Carolina (Blue Cross NC) and is effective the first day of employment. The Town currently pays 100% of the cost for full time employees and a portion of the cost for dependent coverage.  Insurance is available for part time employees. The amount paid depends upon the number of hours worked

Dental insurance is administered by Blue Cross Blue Shield of North Carolina (Blue Cross NC) and is effective the first day of employment. The Town currently pays 100% of the cost for full-time employees and a portion of the cost for dependent coverage. Insurance is available for part-time employees. The amount paid depends upon the number of hours worked.

Vision insurance is administered by Blue Cross Blue Shield of North Carolina (Blue Cross NC) and is effective the first day of employment. Vision coverage is automatically included as part of the Town’s health insurance plan at no additional cost to employees.
Sick Leave: Employees in full or part time regular positions earn 12 days per year.
Paid Holidays: There are 12 official Town holidays.  Employees in full or part time regular positions are eligible for paid holiday time.
Credit Union: Employees of the Town of Chapel Hill are eligible to open accounts at the Civic Federal Credit Union.
Parking is provided free of charge at or near worksite.
Tuition assistance is available upon completion of the initial probationary period.

You don't need a membership to enjoy our aquatics facilities, but you do need to pay for daily admission or a swim pass. The cost of each admission or pass varies according to residency and age group. Here's what to know:
You can pay a daily admission fee each time you swim.
We have Family Pass Packages that apply only to the 6-month pass and annual pass.
An annual pass is valid for one year from date of purchase.
Additional Family Member : families who qualify for a Family Pass Package may add additional family member minor children who live in the same household as Additional Family Member for additional fee
If lightning is detected within a 10-mile radius, lifeguards will sound a long whistle blast to signal immediate suspension of all pool activities and to clear the pool deck.
The pool deck and showers will reopen only after 30 minutes have passed with no lightning.

You can rent many of our facilities when when we're not using them for our programs. To do so, complete a rental application and pay the appropriate fee.
Submit your request at least two weeks before you need it.
We will accept rental applications for all facilities up to six months in advance of the requested rental date except for athletic playing fields.
Rental Fees vary according to the facility and are due at the time of rental request.

We'll collect a security damage deposit of $100 for all facility rentals. Meeting room rentals by non-profit groups when offering programs of a non-commercial nature that are open to the public and benefit the community at large are exempt from the $100 deposit requirement.
