More Information about Orange County Taxes How to estimate your tax bill for 2025-26 Residents are required to report to the county tax office by January 31 each year their taxable personal property (such as boats, business equipment, etc.) Motor vehicles need not be reported to the County for property tax purposes if already registered with the State. A resident of North Carolina who owns a motor vehicle here is required to register the vehicle with the NC Division of Motor Vehicles and to pay Town and County taxes on the vehicle. Property taxes on real estate and certain personal property other than motor vehicles are billed in the summer each year and include Town of Chapel Hill taxes, County taxes and, for residents of the Chapel Hill-Carrboro school district in southeast Orange County, a supplemental School District tax. Property taxes must be paid by January 5th to avoid penalties. Property taxes and the Town's license taxes on vehicles are billed by the Department of Motor Vehicles at the same time as the renewal of State vehicle registration. Orange County residents may pay real property tax bills in person at the Revenue Office on the bottom floor of the Chapel Hill Town Hall (405 Martin Luther King Jr. Blvd.) or in person at the Orange County Tax Office located in the Gateway Center in downtown Hillsborough. Taxes for Chapel Hill residents in Durham County may be paid by mail to the Durham Tax Collection Office, 200 East Main Street, Durham (919-560-0300) or in person at the Durham County Tax Office in Durham. Property taxes are collected for the Town's service year beginning on July 1. For example, the Town property taxes billed in the summer of 2025 to be paid by January 5, 2026, will pay for services from July 1, 2025, through June 30, 2026 (the 2025-2026 service year). The Town of Chapel Hill property tax rate for the 2025-2026 service year is 50.0 cents per $100 of taxable value.

Skip to main content Cedar Falls Park Located in northern Chapel Hill, Cedar Falls Park features multiple athletic fields and courts, a fully-equipped playground, and a creek-side trail. Parking Lot Improvements June 2026 Update Starting the week of June 8, contractors will be on site to pave the current gravel and dirt areas in phases. We expect them to be done the first week of July, weather permitting. At least two-thirds of the lot will be open at all times to minimize the impact on parking. Cedar Falls Park Tennis Court Reconstruction Project June 2026 Update The project is substantially complete, and we look forward to opening the Cedar Falls Park tennis courts this summer. The court surface coatings are complete (courts will be striped for tennis play only), the plaza and bleachers are onsite for completion. The path lighting as well as the new sustainable LED lighting for the courts is complete.  We've experienced recent delays with relocating underground electrical lines and are working diligently with Duke Energy to wrap this up. Once the electrical lines are relocated, final steps are to complete the installation of the American Disabilities Act (ADA) compliant sidewalk and landscaping. Home Government Departments Fire-Rescue Welcome to the Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department! The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department has a proud tradition of service to the community since 1927. The department provides fire suppression, emergency medical services, and light technical rescue services. The department also has a comprehensive Community Risk Reduction program to help keep our community safe. Does your department check and install car seats for children? Yes! Fill out the Child Passenger Safety Inspection Request form to schedule an appointment, or visit our Child Car Seat Installation page for more information. How do I order a reflective address sign? Reflective address signs are very important for emergency services. If we can't find you, we can't help you. Locating addresses can be especially tricky at night. Order a reflective address sign today. Signs are only $15 or $20 if mounted to a metal post. Use this form to order your reflective address sign. The signs are made at the fire-rescue department and you will be contacted once it is ready for pick-up. Does your department check and install smoke alarms and/or carbon monoxide detectors? As a part of our Community Risk Reduction program the Fire-Rescue Department installs smoke and carbon monoxide alarms for residents of Carrboro and the South Orange Fire District. Smoke alarms provide early notifications increasing the likelihood that you and your family will be able to escape if there is a fire in your home. Submit this form to schedule installation or inspection of a smoke or carbon monoxide alarm. How can I apply for a fire prevention permit? The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department issues construction and operational permits in accordance with applicable state laws and the currently adopted edition of the North Carolina Fire Code. For more information regarding permits, reference Section 105 of the North Carolina Fire Code and in Chapter 12, Article II, Section 12-10 of the Town Code. You can apply for a fire prevention permit by submitting this form . Reminder: Any plans for review (electrical, remodeling, fire alarm, sprinkler system) should be submitted to Town of Carrboro Planning, Zoning, and Inspections through PIPER prior to contacting the Fire-Rescue Department. How to I schedule a fire prevention education event or fire station tour? Birthday Party? Other events? Public Education Event We provide public education events, including fire prevention presentations. Use this form to request a public education event for your group or organization. Station Tours We regularly give station tours and safety talks to all types of groups, both formal and informal. Some of the groups we have had come through in the past include school classes, girl scouts, boy scouts, church groups, day care groups, after school programs, summer camp groups, home school students, and many individuals. Use this form to request a fire station tour. Birthday and Block Parties If you are planning a community block party or birthday party our department will stop by during the event with a fire truck for all to see up close - both adults and children. We can show and explain the use and importance of the many pieces of equipment, display our gear, discuss fire safety topics, and distribute fire safety information. Use this form to request the department come out to your birthday or block party. Other Events View our Community Services page to see what other services are offered by the fire-rescue department. Forms are available to request all of the services offered by the department. View All FAQs /FAQ.aspx

Home Government Departments Police Police Department ABOUT THE DEPARTMENT The Carrboro Police Department protects the citizens, businesses and properties within the municipal limits of the Town of Carrboro by providing committed law enforcement officers and community services that foster a safe living environment in our community. Our headquarters are located in the heart of downtown Carrboro in the landmark Century Center Building at 100 N. Greensboro St. Our department employs 43 people (including 39 full-time, sworn law enforcement officers) functioning in various roles within the department. We are equipped to respond to emergency and non-emergency situations 24-hours a day, 7-days a week, 365-days a year. We have uniform patrol officers, investigators, community services officers, school resource officers and program support staff who work tirelessly to help make our department and community a better, safer place to live and work! Ever wondered what we're all about? Take a few moments to read our Mission, Vision and Values . CALLING 911 In an emergency or life-threatening situation: CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY! Carrboro Police officers are typically dispatched through Orange County Communications, our 911 call center. Do not hesitate calling 911 if your situation requires the immediate response of a police officer. While calling 911 can be intimidating, if you need any type of police assistance, call 911. This is the quickest and most effective way for officers to assist you. If you are unsure as to whether to call 911 or not call 911, CALL 911! Email, SMS and Phone Call Alert System - OC Alerts Did you know that the Carrboro Police Department has an email alert system to notify residents and businesses about criminal activity? Sign up now . The Town of Carrboro is also participating in OCAlerts, which will allow emergency agencies to alert you in the event of a major incident in your area. Click the picture below to register for that service if you wish.

Emergency information and resources are available below.
Please visit the Carrboro Newsroom for updates on Winter Weather.
Orange County Emergency Services responds to emergencies within the Town of Carrboro.

Please report non-emergency storm damage to Orange County. Orange County Emergency Services collects this data to determine the type and severity of weather events. It also helps to identify sever structure damage that may need further assistance.
Learn more about when to call 911 and what to expect when you call .
For downed trees and other debris in a roadway, ensure you are in a safe location, and then call 911.
For downed trees on private property, contact the primary insurer of the property, and then contact a private debris removal company.
Report flooding issues to the Carrboro Stormwater Division .

OC Alerts is Orange County's mass notification system. Sign up here . OC Alerts can contact individuals and businesses through a number of methods. Community members will receive a message through the voice or text communication methods of their choosing. The system is only used to deliver emergency information. Contact information is protected and will not be used for any other purpose.

Weather conditions sometimes prevent garbage collection or other residential curbside services. The town uses the following means to communicate weather-related changes to town services:

Several government agencies offer online disaster information Food safety after power outages — Information is available from the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services . Disaster response — Information on disaster response is available from the American Red Cross in English and Spanish .
Disaster response — Information on disaster response is available from the American Red Cross in English and Spanish .
Extreme cold and power outages — Information related to indoor and outdoor personal safety is available from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention website .
Fire safety during cold weather — Tips are available from the National Fire Protection Association website .
See the documents below for additional information from the town.

When an emergency happens, your family may be without power or access to grocery stores, running water or other day-to-day necessities. The best way to prepare for this possibility is to build a family emergency kit. The kit will be helpful whether you are staying at home, or you have to leave on short notice.
Your family may be split up when disaster strikes or split up as a result. The best way to ensure contact is to make a plan for how you will check in and get back together.
There are many ways to become better prepared for an emergency response.
Store a battery-powered radio or a radio that can be powered with a generator. News is available from several area stations:
Tips for Communicating from Ready NC

Orange County officials work with the Orange County Chapter of the American Red Cross to open emergency shelters when the need arises.  Learn more about emergency shelters in Orange County at readyorange.gov .
Those needing special assistance in a disaster should call 919-245-2884 to register for assistance before a disaster strikes.
Shelter locations may change depending on the emergency.
Shelters will have food and water but will not have easy access to medications or other personal needs.
Smoking is not allowed inside the shelter.

Forming a plan for your pets is the best way to make sure they remain safe during severe weather. See the American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals' website for information on disaster planning with pets. Orange County has the following regulations for animals at shelters: Service animals for the hearing and visually impaired will be allowed in shelters. All other animals are welcome at shelters, and Orange County Animal Services staff will be on hand to board pets in the best manner possible.

Home Government Engage With Us Advisory Boards & Commissions Advisory Boards & Commissions Application for Advisory Boards & Commissions CLICK HERE TO APPLY NOW Applying to serve on an advisory board is the first step! After you submit your application, the Town Clerk will forward it to the advisory board chair and staff liaison.  You will then be contacted by the chair and asked to attend an upcoming meeting of the advisory board. This will allow you to more fully understand the work and commitment of the board. After you have attended a meeting, the chair will submit forms indicating such. Those forms, along with your application, will be reviewed by the Town Council. The Town Council appoint citizens to serve on advisory boards and commissions. These volunteers perform a vital role in our community by contributing their time, expertise and talent to the Town. As advisory board members, they serve willingly and without compensation. They interpret town codes, they counsel and advise elected officials and they listen to citizen appeals. Check to see if your residence is in Carrboro’s jurisdiction Advisory Board Documents Rules of Procedure for Town of Carrboro Boards and Commissions - amended 9-13-2022 Town of Carrboro Advisory Board Recruitment and Appointment Policy Advisory Board Chair Forms 1) Advisory Board Chair Applicant Summary and Contact Form (Complete one per applicant) Advisory Boards Training Video View the online Advisory Boards training video . All new advisory board members are required to complete training by watching the training video provided by the UNC SOG. After completion of the video, please notify the Town Clerk or your advisory board chair. As part of the Town’s commitment to engage with all members of the public, it is working to reduce the communication gap for residents who speak a language other than English through the Town of Carrboro Language Access Plan (LAP) , adopted by the Carrboro Town Council on April 9, 2024. The Town aims to better serve the community and advance language access efforts that make the Town a more welcoming and inclusive place for all.
Requesting an Interpreter or Interpretation Equipment View the tabs below for details on how to request an interpreter or interpretation equipment.
View the tabs below for details on how to request an interpreter or interpretation equipment.

When Visiting Town Facilities When Attending a Town Event, Program or Council Meeting When Visiting Town Facilities At several of our public-facing Town facilities including Town Hall and the Carrboro Century Center, you will now see signs to let visitors know that free interpretation services are available—all you need to do is ask a staff member! When Attending a Town Event, Program or Council Meeting When attending a Town event/program either virtually or in person, please contact organizers at least 48 hours in advance to ensure an interpreter is present. For interpretation assistance at a Town Council Meeting or Work Session, please email the request and meeting date to communications@CarrboroNC.gov. This notification must be sent at least ten calendar days in advance.

At several of our public-facing Town facilities including Town Hall and the Carrboro Century Center, you will now see signs to let visitors know that free interpretation services are available—all you need to do is ask a staff member!

When attending a Town event/program either virtually or in person, please contact organizers at least 48 hours in advance to ensure an interpreter is present. For interpretation assistance at a Town Council Meeting or Work Session, please email the request and meeting date to communications@CarrboroNC.gov. This notification must be sent at least ten calendar days in advance.
When attending a Town event/program either virtually or in person, please contact organizers at least 48 hours in advance to ensure an interpreter is present.

For interpretation assistance at a Town Council Meeting or Work Session, please email the request and meeting date to communications@CarrboroNC.gov. This notification must be sent at least ten calendar days in advance.
All Town Staff have access to a language line to access an interpreter over the phone. When on the phone with staff, inform them of the language you speak and that an interpreter is needed.
Simultaneous interpretation is used for large group meetings in which the speaker does not pause, and interpretation is delivered in real time.
There is no charge to borrow equipment.
Please submit requests at least 5 days in advance. Requests made later than 5 days before will be met to the best of our ability.
We require first-time borrowers to schedule a 15-minute appointment with staff to review Interpretation Equipment Borrowing Policies and equipment instructions before picking up the equipment.
Once you pick up the equipment, you are responsible for the equipment and may not loan it to a third party.
If the damages are irreparable or otherwise impractical to attempt a repair, you may be responsible for the item's replacement cost.
Complete the Interpretation Equipment Request and Agreement Form .

Any resident who wishes to file a formal complaint with the Town that alleges discrimination on the basis of limited English Proficient (LEP) status or noncompliance with the Town's Language Access Plan should submit a Language Access Formal Complaint Form.

Home Government Departments Town Manager Climate Action Climate Action The Town of Carrboro is a national leader in climate action, prioritizing strategies that increase the resilience of Black, Indigenous, and People of Color, low-income and other communities disproportionately impacted by climate change. The Carrboro Connects Comprehensive Plan , Carrboro’s blueprint for the future, is built on this foundation of race and equity and climate action. These themes are intentionally woven into every aspect on the plan, including affordable housing, transportation and mobility, green infrastructure, economic sustainability, and land use. This guiding document builds upon bold action plans, starting with one of the country’s first comprehensive climate action plans which was adopted by the Town in 2009 and carrying through to the updated and re-adopted Community Climate Action Plan . In 2024, Carrboro created a Climate Action Team within the Office of the Town Manager to accelerate progress on the Town’s climate action goals to achieve 80% reduction in community-wide and municipal greenhouse gas emissions, as compared to a 2010 baseline, by 2030. The Town's Three Main Climate Action Objectives: 1. Reduce municipal greenhouse gas emissions by increasing energy efficiency in Town buildings, electrifying the Town fleet, and adding more renewable energy to Town facilities 2. Reduce community greenhouse gas emissions by increasing access to renewable energy and energy efficiency programs for all residents and businesses, developing programs that encourage more residents to choose more environmentally friendly transportation options and reduce waste, and adopting a Unified Development Ordinance that encourages smart, climate friendly development 3. Develop a system-wide approach to build resilience to the inevitable impacts of climate change with a focus on low-income, historically marginalized neighborhoods disproportionately impacted by urban heat islands and flooding Get Involved Apply to be appointed to the Climate and Environmental Advisory Commission Carrboro Community Climate Action Plan Revisions Community Climate Action Plan - Updated Oct 2020 ECPP and CCAP Update - November 2022 CCAP and ECPP Update - February 2022 CCAP and ECPP Update - October 2021 ECPP and CCAP Update June 2021 ECPP and CCAP Update January 2021 CCAP and ECPP Update - October 2020 ECPP and CCAP Update January 2020 ECPP and CCAP Update October 2019 ECPP and CCAP Update June 2019 Contact Us Amy  Armbruster Chief Sustainability Officer Email Amy Armbruster More Information Government Websites by CivicPlus®

Home Government Departments Town Manager Race and Equity Race and Equity in Carrboro Vision Statement The Town of Carrboro envisions being a community where race does not determine outcomes and all have equitable opportunities and resources.  We envision a time when participation in community events, programs and advisory boards represent community demographics.  All will feel safe, secure and know their voice is valued.  We strive to be an inclusive and open-minded organization that has a culture created by its diverse staff, which serves the public through a social (racial) justice lens. Race & Equity Action Plan (REAP) The 2023 REAP provides an overview of the Town of Carrboro’s Race and Equity Journey and a blueprint for advancing the work through 2025. The Town is currently working on an updated REAP. Our Collaborators Government Alliance On Race and Equity (GARE) - All jurisdictions in Orange County are members of GARE, a national network of governments working to dismantle strctural inequity to achieve racial equity and advance opportunities for all. One Orange Countywide Racial Equity Framework - Dashboard used by Carrboro, Chapel Hill, Hillsborough, and Orange County showing at-a-glance views of racial equity in Orange County. It contains statistics on public health, education and economic opportunity as well as those related to living conditions, infrastructure and voting. Government Websites by CivicPlus®

Carrboro is among 112 localities designated as a Silver-level Bicycle Friendly Community , by the League of American Bicyclists. The Town was first awarded the designation in 2010, and most recently renewed its status in 2024.

ReCYCLEry NC , a non-profit based in Chapel Hill, accepts donated bicycles and offers workshops to teach community members bicycle repair and maintenance skills as they work towards earning their own bikes. Both children and adults can participate in these workshops.

Chapel Hill Transit and GoTriangle buses offer front bike racks with spaces for two bikes. Visit GoTriangle's Bikes on Buses to learn how to use the bus bike rack. There is also a demonstration station at the West Main Street at Carrboro Town Hall Eastbound bus stop for residents to practice using the bus bike rack.
BikeWalkNC is a statewide advocacy organization for bicyclists and pedestrians. They publish and compile bicycle safety and education resources .

The League of American Bicyclists is a membership organization that promotes cycling for fun, fitness and transportation through advocacy and education. They publish a number of "smart cycle" resources for new and experienced bicyclists.
Safe Routes to School is a Town of Carrboro program which aims to enhance safety and convenience for students walking, biking, and rolling to school.

Brief History of Carrboro Originally known as West End, due to its geographic location directly west of Chapel Hill, Carrboro was first settled in 1882 around a University of North Carolina railroad spur. In 1911, the town was incorporated under the name Venable, for chemistry professor and University of North Carolina president, Francis Preston Venable. In 1913, the state legislature renamed the town Carrboro at request of Julian S. Carr, a post-Civil War business leader and owner of the local textile mill. Carr was also an active and influential participant in Jim Crow era efforts to create a system of racial segregation. Although the town continues to bear his name, the values and actions of Carr do not represent Carrboro today. For the first fifty years after its incorporation, Carrboro remained a small mill town with a slow, steady pace of growth. In 1960, approximately 2,000 people lived in the town. In the late 1960s the town’s population began to increase stemming from the growth occurring at UNC-Chapel Hill and area businesses. Growth has continued through present day, resulting in the creation of a vibrant and diverse community including the Carrboro Farmer’s Market and two venues that host national music acts (the ArtsCenter and Cat’s Cradle). The town also hosts annual events like the Carrboro Music Festival and Carrboro Film Festival. Today, over 20,000 people call Carrboro home.

Begin your self-guided tour at 301 West Main Street. Use our Google Map above as a guide or follow along on a Strava Route here created by Fleet Feet.
Download a PDF version of the Walking Tour Map .

Carrboro Civic Club: The Carrboro Civic Club was originally founded in 1945 under the name of the Carrboro Club for Town Improvement and Beautification. The building, owned by Carrboro Wolen Mills, originally served as an office building  for the mill. It  then was donated to the Chapel Hill Junior Service League, who in turn donated the building to the Carrboro Civic Club in 1945.  The Civic Club used the building as a free public library for many years. They also provided a free children's medical clinic until they were forced by the Public Health Department to close in 1960. In 2001, the Carrboro Civic Club donated the building to the Town of Carrboro, which uses it for community programming and as a voting precinct.

Historical Marker Honoring Manly McCauley : Through this marker erected in February 2024, the Orange County Community Remembrance Coalition, the Equal Justice Initiative and the Town of Carrboro commemorate the life and tragic death of Manly McCauley, a Black man who suffered a white mob lynching in October 1898, just west of Carrboro, N.C.

Artist Matthew Willey, founder of The Good of the Hive Initiative, stopped in Carrboro on his trek to paint 50,000 honeybees around the world in 2016. This mural is painted on the side of the Fire Station. Learn more about Willey and all his murals .

The land for this cemetery was donated to the town by Dr. Foy Robertson. The cemetery has been in use since the early 1910s, with the oldest marked stone being at 1915. There are several unmarked stones that may date back further. This is Carrboro’s oldest cemetery.

This church building in the second district of the African Methodist Episcopal Church (AME) in Chapel Hill, North Carolina in the United States. Being the first black church in the area, it is the spiritual home to numerous communicants, including numerous students studying at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.

The body shop located at 405 E. Main St. is owned and operated by Brother Peacemaker. Brother Peacemaker grew up as Fred Marsh just down the road in Pittsboro. He changed his name after a near fatal car accident spared him his life, and that of his buddy, and awakened in him a calling: he needed to spread peace. Brother Peacemaker not only fixes vehicles in his tiny body shop on Main Street in Carrboro, he ministers to anyone needing a little touch-up of their own.  Learn more about Brother Peacemaker and the Gates of Beauty .

Butterflies Mural : Muralist Loren Pease completed the butterfly mural on the side of Gray Squirrel Coffee Co. at 360 E. Main St. in September 2023. The mural shows a scene of butterflies migrating, seemingly off the building and toward downtown Carrboro. They fly out of and over three gold frames, which Pease describes as a nod to FRANK Gallery across the street.

The original building on this site was erected in the 1880’s, but was destroyed by fire in 1916. The building was rebuilt shortly after and is still standing today. The building was originally a gristmill and cotton gin, and has been the site of many businesses since.

(Erected in 2013 by North Carolina Department of Archives and History.) The Elizabeth Cotten Historical Marker is at the intersection of East Main Street and Roberson Street, on the right when traveling west on East Main Street. Learn more about the Elizabeth Cotten Historical Marker .

This building is currently home to the Carrboro Police Department. Constructed in the 1920s, this building was originally the Carrboro Baptist Church. In 1997, the building was purchased by the Town of Carrboro and opened in 2000 as the Carrboro Century Center.
These streets encompass much of Carrboro’s original residential district. These typical mill houses are good examples of the architectural style of the 1920s and 1930s.
This business was founded in 1898 and opened in Carrboro in 1923. The railroad and the mill allowed Fitch Lumber to thrive and is one of Carrboro’s original and longest-lasting businesses.

Carrboro is celebrating Juneteenth all weekend. Check out some of the other events happening around town.

Thursday, June 19, is annually proclaimed Juneteenth National Independence Day in Carrboro by Mayor Barbara Foushee, who encourages all residents to carry the spirit of Juneteenth with them all year long and learn more about the history of Juneteenth.

Elizabeth Cotten Family Presents Historic Martin Guitar to Town of Carrboro
Elizabeth Cotten Family Presents Historic Martin Guitar to Town of Carrboro Carrboro Town News Posted on May 18, 2026
The instrument was formally accepted by Carrboro Mayor Barbara Foushee and Town Manager Patrice Toney on behalf of the community.

The guitar’s presentation marks a permanent homecoming for an instrument connected to the musical legacy that helped shape the internationally recognized “Cotten Picking” style and influenced generations of folk musicians worldwide. The guitar is a limited-edition Martin 150th anniversary model created in honor of the iconic artist and was originally gifted to Ms. Cotten by the U.S. Congress in 1983.
Photos by Jonathan Drake for the Town of Carrboro

Brenda Evans and her son, Derrick Fennell Jr., presented the guitar on behalf of the Cotten family during the evening’s festivities at Carrboro Town Commons to Town Manager Patrice Toney and Mayor Barbara Foushee.
Read the summary at C arrboro Town Council Adopts FY 2026-27 Budget, Maintains Tax Rate and Invests in Community Priorities

Voted to reduce the speed limit from 45 to 35 miles per hour for a short section of Homestead Road near Stratford Drive within Carrboro town limits. This is the first step in the process to codify a 35 mile per hour speed limit for the entire Homestead Road corridor, a state-maintained road. Changing the speed limit for a state-maintained road involves the approval of an NCDOT ordinance. A resurfacing project for Homestead Road is anticipated to begin this summer. It will include new pavement marking plans to reduce the width of the travel lanes and increase the width of the shoulder.  These changes are designed to improve safety by reducing vehicle speeds and creating better infrastructure for cyclists.
Received a presentation recapping the Savannah Bananas event and an overview about Local Tourism Agencies.

Regular business meetings are generally held the first and third Tuesdays of the month. Work sessions are generally held the second Tuesdays of the month. Public comment is not accepted during work sessions, and votes are not taken.

Home News Flash Town of Carrboro Selects Latasha “Tasha” Pegues as Human Resources Director News Flash Town of Carrboro Selects Latasha “Tasha” Pegues as Human Resources Director Carrboro Town News Posted on June 09, 2026 CARRBORO, NC – Town Manager Patrice Toney has announced the selection of Latasha “Tasha” Pegues as the new Human Resources Director for the Town of Carrboro. Pegues is an accomplished human resources leader with more than 23 years of experience serving organizations in local government, education, and healthcare. She currently serves as a Senior Human Resources Business Partner with City of Raleigh and is scheduled to begin her new role with the Town of Carrboro on June 29, 2026. “We are thrilled to welcome Tasha to the Town of Carrboro,” said Town Manager Patrice Toney. “Her extensive experience in public-sector human resources makes her an outstanding addition to our executive team and will help strengthen our workforce while advancing our vision for a thriving, diverse, and supportive workplace culture.” “I am honored to join the Town of Carrboro and look forward to partnering with Town leadership and employees to support the Town's mission, values, and strategic priorities,” Pegues said. “I am excited to contribute to Carrboro's continued success by strengthening organizational culture, investing in employee development, and advancing human resources initiatives that support both employees and the community we serve.” In addition to her current position with the City of Raleigh, Pegues has held human resources leadership roles with Wake County Government, Raleigh Oaks Behavioral Health in Garner, the North Carolina Department of Commerce, Murdoch Developmental Center in Butner, and Marlboro County School District in Bennettsville, S.C. Her professional experience includes employee relations, organizational development, leadership coaching, talent management, workplace investigations, policy development, and strategic workforce planning. Pegues holds a Master of Arts in Human Resource Management from American Public University, a Bachelor of Science in Sociology from Grand Canyon University, and a graduate certificate in Organizational Management and Human Capital Leadership. She is also an International Public Management Association for Human Resources Senior Certified Professional (IPMA-SCP), a credential that reflects advanced expertise in public-sector human resources leadership. The Town of Carrboro extends its gratitude to Wendi Calvert for her dedicated service as Interim Human Resources Director during the recruitment and transition process. Home News Flash Traffic Alert – Greensboro Street Resurfacing News Flash Traffic Alert – Greensboro Street Resurfacing Carrboro Town News Posted on June 08, 2026 A resurfacing project on Greensboro Street from Braxton Foushee Street to Oak Avenue will be underway beginning Sunday, June 14 with lane closures and operations occurring nightly from 7 p.m. to 6 a.m. Sunday through Thursday. The project will include milling and paving work, as well as a new pavement marking plan that will reduce the width of vehicle travel lanes and add a new buffer for bicycle lanes. The contractor for the NC Department of Transportation project is Fred Smith Company. Please be alert when traveling through the area as lane closures will be implemented nightly; there will be a pilot vehicle for maneuvering traffic through the work area. The project will be underway for three to four weeks, depending on weather conditions. Home News Flash Westwood Cemetery Improvements Underway this June News Flash Westwood Cemetery Improvements Underway this June Carrboro Town News Posted on June 08, 2026 | Last Updated on June 08, 2026 Beginning June 15, visitors to Westwood Cemetery will notice construction activities as the Town implements improvements identified in the Westwood Cemetery Master Plan. The current project includes: Reconfiguring the cemetery access road to improve visitor safety Installing a new asphalt walking path Constructing a rain garden and drainage swale to better manage stormwater and reduce erosion Westwood Cemetery will remain open throughout construction. Visitors are asked to be mindful of work zones and exercise caution when visiting the cemetery. Thank you for your patience as we complete these improvements. About Westwood Cemetery Established around 1933, Westwood Cemetery is an 8.73-acre municipal burial ground located at 401 Davie Road. Westwood is one of two active town-owned cemeteries (alongside Old Carrboro Cemetery) managed by the Public Works Department. In 2023, the Carrboro Town Council adopted the Westwood Cemetery Master Plan. This expansion outlines additions of walking paths, rain gardens, a memorial wall, and a loop road. For more information visit https://www.carrboronc.gov/2966/Westwood-Cemetery-Master-Plan or contact PWProjects@carrboronc.gov . Prior to joining Carrboro, Toney served as Assistant City Manager for the City of Winston-Salem beginning in 2016, overseeing major operational areas including public safety, emergency management, housing development, neighborhood services, and human relations. She also served as Budget and Evaluation Director for five years and previously served in leadership and budget roles with Forsyth County Government, building a distinguished career spanning more than two decades in public service.

Toney holds a Master of Public Affairs degree from the University of North Carolina at Greensboro and a Bachelor of Arts degree from the University of North Carolina at Charlotte. She has also completed executive leadership programs through the Harvard Kennedy School and the UNC School of Government.

Carrboro Celebrates Police Week CARRBORO, NC – The week of May 10-16, 2026 is recognized as Police Week in Carrboro, and Friday, May 15 is Peace Officers Memorial Day in Carrboro, as proclaimed by Mayor Barbara Foushee. Residents are encouraged to share their gratitude with the officers and other professionals in the Police Department for promoting the safety of Carrboro’s residents and visitors, and to understand the duties and responsibilities of these essential employees and the risks they often must face to help make Carrboro a safe and welcoming place to live. Peace Officers Memorial Day honors law enforcement officers who have been killed or disabled in the line of duty. Employees in the Carrboro Police Department provide a vital public service by acting as first responders in emergency and crisis situations; by investigating and solving crimes; by offering a variety of community services to individuals, local businesses, and community organizations and agencies; and by participating in ongoing training to stay up to date on current policing techniques, to achieve the department’s bias-free policing goals, and to provide community services in a fair and professional manner. Access the complete proclamation at https://www.carrboronc.gov/2630/Proclamations-and-Resolutions Check out these interesting facts about the Carrboro Police Department in 2025: The Police Department handled more than 16,000 calls for service and hosted 78 community events, including National Night Out, neighborhood meetings, job fairs and prescription drug drop-off events. The Police Department continued to use naloxone to reverse opioid overdoses Employees in the Police Department attended over 2,500 hours of training; one officer completed crisis intervention training; and five new officers were hired Officer Hunter Tyson was named Carrboro’s 2025 Police Officer of the Year A Crisis Response Social Worker was hired to assist our Diversion Social Worker.  Their roles assist to deflect low-level offenses from the Criminal Justice System and support community members in need. In 2025 officers deflected 258 criminal offenses from the Criminal Justice System. The Carrboro Police Department consistently ranks among the major categories of Town services that have the highest levels of satisfaction in the Carrboro Community Survey. Members of Patrol Platoon 4 demonstrate the community-focused service provided by the Carrboro Police Department. They were recognized by the police department’s social workers for having the most referrals for service and support. Carrboro Town News Posted on May 13, 2026

Period supplies are a necessary product, each month, for millions of people across the country, however national surveys and research studies report that one in four menstruating individuals struggle to secure enough period supplies to meet their needs each month due to a lack of income.
Period poverty exacerbates the vicious cycle of poverty by forcing one in five menstruators to withdraw from daily life, losing pay or missing educational opportunities.

Carrboro is proud to be a home to trusted community-based organizations, including Period Power, a program of the Diaper Bank of North Carolina, that recognizes the importance of period products in ensuring health and providing economic stability, and distributes period products through various channels

The Town of Carrboro is grateful for period supply banks, their staff, volunteers and donors. Carrboro residents are encouraged to donate generously to supply banks, product drives and those organizations that collect and distribute period products to those that are struggling with period poverty so that all of Carrboro’s people can thrive and reach their full potential.

Multiple units sustained significant fire damage, with additional smoke damage to adjacent units. At least 20 residents have been displaced. American Red Cross personnel are assisting displaced residents with temporary housing and immediate needs. Authorities are withholding the fatality victim’s name pending notification of the next of kin.
The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

CARRBORO, NC – Mayor Barbara Foushee has proclaimed May 3-9, 2026 to be Small Business Week in Carrboro and encourages all residents to visit and shop in local small businesses during this week and throughout the year.

The Carrboro Economic Development department supports the business community, with a special emphasis on locally owned businesses, by serving as an information hub to help businesses succeed. With planning, funding, and networking resources on hand, entrepreneurs and business owners at any point in their journey are encouraged to reach out and get connected.

We are officially in the month of May with lots of great things coming your way.  We are celebrating Mothers and Mother Figures, Cinco de Mayo, and we are raising awareness about mental health.  There are also some other important items that I want to call your attention to.

The Annual Budget Process is underway and I encourage residents to stay engaged.  The Town Manager will present the recommended Town Budget and Capital Improvement Plan (CIP) presentation on May 12th during a council work session.  This will be everyone’s first look at these documents.  There are a couple of opportunities for community to provide input during public hearings on the following dates:  May 19th and June 2nd.  All meetings are at Carrboro Town Hall and start at 6 pm.  The town’s annual budget process includes determining funding for services and infrastructure as well as balancing community needs with available revenue.  The overall process is grounded in transparency, public participation and alignment with community values.  We look forward to hearing from community.

Freight Train Blues , Carrboro’s signature music event returns for its 12th year and we are super excited to celebrate our very own Elizabeth Cotten, who was a pioneering folk and blues artist from Carrboro.  These are family-friendly, outdoor events that will bring us together to enjoy some great music. Our opening concert on May 15th will feature a special presentation from descendants of Elizabeth Cotten including Brenda Evans, the great granddaughter of Ms. Cotten. Don’t miss it!

We are officially in Bike Month in Carrboro with a mix of social rides, a bike breakfast, workshops and other events.  We encourage residents to use bikes for transit and to support bike-friendly infrastructure and policies.  This initiative is directly tied to our climate action goals, aiming to reduce car use and emissions while promoting healthy living.  Here is a short list of events:
As always, I look forward to seeing you around town anytime.  It continues to be an honor to serve our community.

Carrboro Mayor Barbara Foushee and Chapel Hill Mayor Jess Anderson welcomed the group, highlighting the essential role small businesses play in sustaining the local economy. Carrboro Mayor Pro-Tem Eliazar Posada-Orozco and Chapel Hill Mayor Pro-Tem Camille Berry also took to the stage to share remarks expressing their appreciation for the business community.

Carrboro Economic Development Director Jon Hartman-Brown and Chapel Hill Economic Development Director David Putnam then recognized 23 businesses for their milestone achievements ranging from five years to an impressive 119 years in operation. Together, the businesses represented more than six centuries of collective commitment to Chapel Hill and Carrboro.

The celebration kicked off the start of Small Business Week, taking place May 3-9. During this week, the Town of Carrboro encourages all residents to support local businesses and celebrate the vital contributions of entrepreneurs and small business owners to the community’s long-term economic resilience.
Mayor Foushee will be directly supporting various upcoming events and invites community participation in the following:
Participating in Bike Month events including Bike and Roll to School Day on May 6, the Bike Breakfast on May 12, and Bike to Work Day on May 15.
Participating and supporting the Orange County Senior Games which are being held April 16 – May 6.
Challenging Town employees to have walking meetings and increase daily activities.
Encouraging children to walk or ride their bikes to school through Safe Routes to School .

The Move with the Mayor® Spring Mental Health and Physical Activity Challenge is an initiative to promote physical activity while fostering social connections among our residents, directly addressing the need for stronger social bonds and improved health outcomes.

Presented by the Carrboro Police Department and the REACH social worker program that engages in outreach and provides holistic support for community members experiencing mental health crisis, substance addiction and housing needs, this is the first of its kind mental health fair offered locally. Attendees will be able to discuss services with a variety of providers and support groups for people and families affected by mental illness.

Biking is an easy way for people to reduce their carbon footprint and advance the Town’s climate action goals by avoiding the use of single-occupancy vehicles and reducing reliance on non-renewable resources for transportation.

National Bike Month is also an opportunity to experience the joys and health benefits of biking, to get outside during the spring season, and to see and experience the community at a different scale and pace.
Did you know? Carrboro became the first community in North Carolina to be designated a Silver Level Bicycle Friendly Community by the League of American Bicyclists.
How to evaluate the bike you have, and adjustments you can make
A review of laws that apply to bicycles, including how the state defines an electric assisted bicycle
How to compensate for less flexibility and slower reaction times that occur as we age.
Bike and ped resources including repair for bicycles, map and routing resources, and reflective gear and bike lights
Town of Carrboro programs and services including a fire engine!

Carrboro In Motion advances goals of the Inclusive Carrboro Communications and Community Engagement Plan by creating a method for the Town to establish a presence in communities and neighborhoods, build relationships, and offer multiple ways for residents to contribute input and feedback about services and topics.
The Carrboro Town Council met on Tuesday, April 14 and took the following actions:
Authorized granting of a permanent easement on town owned property at 604 Jones Ferry Road
Adopted the National Incident Management System and Incident Command System

Responding crews arrived in just over four minutes and encountered heavy smoke and fire conditions on the second and third floors of a multi-unit apartment building. Due to the volume of fire and the need for additional personnel, a second alarm was requested to bring additional firefighters to the scene. Firefighters initiated an aggressive interior fire attack, and the fire was brought under control within approximately 60 minutes.

Multiple units sustained significant fire damage, with additional smoke damage to adjacent units. A total of 18 residents were displaced. The property management group and the American Red Cross are assisting displaced residents with temporary housing and immediate needs.
One resident was transported with minor injuries. One firefighter was evaluated on scene for minor injuries.

Mayor Barbara Foushee has officially declared April as Earth Month , encouraging all residents and businesses to embrace sustainable choices, participate in local climate initiatives, and advocate for bold legislation to protect our planet.
Jenny Cook and Chris Amsbury will emcee the festivities at Town Hall. Don’t miss Chris Amsbury introducing Beatles cover band One After 919, performing on West Weaver Street at 1 PM.
Carrboro Community Garden returns with free plants to share with the community.
Test your skills on the rock wall near Country Junction on West Weaver Street.
The Town of Carrboro’s Climate Action Team will provide resources and information on sustainability initiatives.
A variety of food trucks will be available at Town Hall to keep you fueled throughout the day.
Mayor Barbara Foushee has proclaimed April as Sexual Assault Awareness Month in the Town of Carrboro.

Over half of women and almost one-third of men in the United States have experienced sexual violence involving physical contact in their lifetimes. Women and racial and ethnic minority groups, trans and gender-nonconforming people, people with disabilities, and children experience a higher burden of sexual violence.
The winning projects from the Town of Carrboro Communication and Engagement Department , staffed by Director Catherine Lazorko and Coordinator Eve Greene, are as follows:

The Town of Carrboro received a first-place award for Photography and a submission titled “Carrboro: Get Pumped!” In May 2025, community came out to view and take a ride on the new and improved pump track at a ribbon cutting held at Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Park. Event photos were taken by Evelyn Greene, communication and engagement coordinator. The drone photo was taken by Robert Douglass, formerly of the information technology department. Judges noted the photography used in social media posts was great quality and helped demonstrate what a pump track is for to those who are not familiar.

The Town of Carrboro also won a second-place award in the category of Crisis Communications for its response to Tropical Storm Chantal, which dropped nearly 10 inches of rain in less than two hours, causing widespread flooding and property damage. The Town delivered timely, accurate and compassionate communications to help the community navigate rapidly changing conditions and provide resources for displaced residents. By meeting residents where they were, online, in person and through trusted community partners, Carrboro ensured that critical information reached those who needed it most.
Winning projects recognized by NC3C were developed by the Town of Carrboro Communication and Engagement Department, staffed by Director Catherine Lazorko and Coordinator Evelyn Greene.
Local News Day on April 9, 2026 was recognized by Mayor Barbara Foushee in an official proclamation.
Local journalism is a foundational element of a functioning and healthy democracy. Americans overwhelmingly say local news and information is the most important and most trusted source of news.

To commemorate the occasion, the Town will host a public planting of a native tree at Wilson Park, 110 Williams St. This year’s celebration holds special significance as Carrboro marks over four decades of continuous recognition as a Tree City USA by the National Arbor Day Foundation.

The preservation of the urban forest is a key initiative identified in both the Carrboro Connects Comprehensive Plan and the Community Climate Action Plan. These plans prioritize data-driven tree planting in historically underserved areas to mitigate the urban heat island effect and ensure equitable access to the physical and mental health benefits provided by nature.
Maintaining a dedicated tree board or department.

To support this effort, we are asking community members to share their own experiences and ideas for improvement. A public survey is now available on the project webpage. The survey takes about 5–10 minutes to complete and is open to everyone, especially people with disabilities and those who support someone with a disability.

Later this spring, the Town intends to host listening sessions and focus groups to gather more in depth feedback from individuals with disabilities, senior citizens, and caregivers. Details about these events and other opportunities to get involved will be shared on the project webpage and through Town communications.
The Town encourages all residents to participate and help make Carrboro a more accessible and inclusive community.

Visitors to downtown Chapel Hill and Carrboro will see yellow banners on light poles and be welcomed with posters, pennants, buttons, and rack cards with Bananas Ball Fan specials at select businesses. There will be designated ambassadors dressed in yellow aprons in both downtowns helping visitors. Cut-outs of the Savannah Bananas mascot “Split” and Chapel Hill Town produced itinerary brochures with the specials, community events and parking details will welcome guests to local hotels.

The Chapel Hill/Orange County Welcome Center at 308 West Franklin Street will be open extended hours on April 11 from 9 am – 5 pm and Sunday April 12 from 9 am – noon. Drop by and take a photo at a Banana selfie station.
The Carrboro Town Council met on Tuesday, March 17 and took the following actions:
Approved a revised Memorandum of Understanding for the Orange County Partnership to End Homelessness
Approved a resolution to authorize a contract for Federal Emergency Management Agency Public Assistance Program Grant Management Services.

Next, the project team will present these recommendations to the Carrboro Town Council at its meeting scheduled Tuesday, April 21 at 6 p.m. for additional input and guidance. Community members can attend in person at Carrboro Town Hall or watch the livestream on the Town’s YouTube channel. Meeting materials are available on the Town website.

Following Council’s review, the project team will begin drafting the updated Action Plan. The Town expects to share a full draft plan with the community in late spring 2026 and will be asking for feedback then.

Music Maker Foundation honors Cotten’s legacy in the world of roots music by emphasizing the cultural diversity, complexity, and vitality of her music and the music of many other artists local to her community and all over the country.

Kelley Breiding is a dynamic multi-instrumentalist and singer hailed by many as "the new queen of country music." A native of North Carolina, she is a sought-after artist whose music is deeply rooted in the traditional sounds of the Blue Ridge mountains. Whether she is showcasing her classic country chops with The Cowboys or performing old-time mountain music with her various ensembles, Kelley Breiding delivers an authentic and captivating performance.

The roots of Gail Ceasar's music runs deep into the Virginia soil. After Music Maker met and began working with the great blues guitarist Boo Hanks in Virginia, we began doing more fieldwork in that state and soon met a blues player from Pittsylvania County named Pete Witcher. We returned several times to record Pete, and every time we visited, Pete made a point of taking us to see his niece Gail Ceasar. He was so proud of her guitar playing. She plays with incredible precision.

Leroy Harper Jr. is not just a saxophonist—he is a living link to the golden age of funk and soul. For over fifteen electrifying years, Leroy held down the saxophone section for the legendary James Brown, touring the world and contributing to the unmistakable sound of The Godfather of Soul. Lynn Grissett is a versatile and highly acclaimed musician, widely recognized for his powerful role in the New Power Generation Hornz, the legendary horn section for Prince. Whether leading his own Quartet or performing with elite ensembles, Lynn Grissett brings a blend of technical mastery, deep musicality, and electrifying stage presence to every engagement.

Sharing bonds of music and blood kinship, the members of the Dedicated Men of Zion bring down a joyfully harmonious version of the gospel in a style so akin to soul music that we refer to it as “sacred soul.”

E. Poplar Roadway Improvements The Town of Carrboro recently completed the repaving of E. Poplar Street as part of the town's annual resurfacing project. This week the final stripes are being placed. The new striping has sharrow symbols placed in areas along the road indicating that the travel way is intended for use by both cars and bicycles. The new striping also has a new wider buffered shoulder providing a safer area for pedestrians to walk.  The design balances the need of all users as well as the overall safety requirements of the emergency services provided by the fire and police departments, and operational requirements of Public Works including solid waste collections on the very narrow one-way road. The Sharrow Symbol means the lane is intended to be shared by cars and bicycles. The project was completed by Daniels Paving and LMJ Pavement Marking with funding through the NC Department of Transportation. Carrboro Town News Posted on March 31, 2026 | Last Updated on March 31, 2026

Earth Hour 2026 aims to be a global reminder of the importance of our planet, the need to protect it, and how little time we have to do so. The event strives to inspire everyone and anyone to feel inspired and empowered to play a part in protecting the planet, no matter how small.
Participation involves two simple actions—turning off lights for one hour and giving an hour for Earth by spending 60 minutes doing something positive for our planet.
Access the livestream direct link to view the meeting on YouTube
The Carrboro Town Council met on Tuesday, March 10 and took the following actions:

Approved the Condominium Agreement and Condominium Deeds with Orange County for the Operation of the Drakeford Library Complex and authorized the Town Manager to sign all required documents, upon final review by the Town Attorney.

Mayor’s Moment – March 2026 I can’t believe we’re already in March—Daylight Saving Time is coming up, and spring is just around the corner. This month brings meaningful observances such as Women’s History Month, International Women's Day, Pi Day, Earth Hour, and Transgender Day of Visibility. Each offers an opportunity to celebrate one another and build meaningful connections within our community. Recently, the council has taken several key actions. One significant decision was to pause the East Weaver Street pilot for one year to allow for additional outreach and engagement with our downtown businesses, patrons, and residents. We believe this additional time will help ensure thoughtful input and collaboration moving forward. The council has also begun budget discussions following our priority-setting retreat and a high-level budget outlook presentation from the town’s finance department. Budget planning begins months before the town manager presents a recommended budget in May 2026. We look forward to robust community input as this important process continues. In addition, we’ve received updates on municipal greenhouse gas emissions and our decarbonization goals as we track progress toward climate targets. We also discussed recovery efforts related to Tropical Storm Chantal, including FEMA public assistance, received an informational presentation on the stormwater enterprise fund program and received updates on transportation projects. I encourage everyone to explore the Town’s new website , where you can find information about council meetings, town projects, community events and festivals. You can also learn more on how to stay engaged and connected through the Stay Engaged section of the website. Constructive relationships between community members and local government make civic engagement not only desirable but essential. By working together, we can foster more equitable and sustainable outcomes and continue improving the quality of life for all. --Mayor Barbara Foushee Carrboro Town News Posted on March 03, 2026 | Last Updated on March 03, 2026

Recently, the council has taken several key actions. One significant decision was to pause the East Weaver Street pilot for one year to allow for additional outreach and engagement with our downtown businesses, patrons, and residents. We believe this additional time will help ensure thoughtful input and collaboration moving forward.

The council has also begun budget discussions following our priority-setting retreat and a high-level budget outlook presentation from the town’s finance department. Budget planning begins months before the town manager presents a recommended budget in May 2026. We look forward to robust community input as this important process continues.

In addition, we’ve received updates on municipal greenhouse gas emissions and our decarbonization goals as we track progress toward climate targets. We also discussed recovery efforts related to Tropical Storm Chantal, including FEMA public assistance, received an informational presentation on the stormwater enterprise fund program and received updates on transportation projects.

I encourage everyone to explore the Town’s new website , where you can find information about council meetings, town projects, community events and festivals. You can also learn more on how to stay engaged and connected through the Stay Engaged section of the website.

Constructive relationships between community members and local government make civic engagement not only desirable but essential. By working together, we can foster more equitable and sustainable outcomes and continue improving the quality of life for all.

Carrboro to survey residents about Town services What do you think of Town services? The Town of Carrboro seeks to find out through a survey being issued to a random sampling of residents. Results are used in Town planning efforts to identify future improvements. The Carrboro Community Survey will be mailed to 2,000 randomly selected Carrboro households. Respondents can complete and return by mail, or take the survey online or by phone. Everyone who completes the survey will be given chance to enter a random drawing for a $500 Visa gift card. For non-English speaking households, foreign language translation will be available in multiple languages. The Town has selected ETC Institute to help with the survey. As a way of thanking everyone who participates, ETC Institute will randomly select one respondent to receive a $500 Visa gift card. A report of the survey findings will be released later this spring. For more information, contact Communication and Engagement Director Catherine Lazorko at clazorko@carrboronc.gov Carrboro Town News Posted on February 27, 2026 | Last Updated on February 27, 2026

Carrboro Announces Redesigned Website CARRBORO, NC – The Town of Carrboro is pleased to announce that it has launched a website redesign that streamlines the homepage with commonly accessed pages, useable and relevant features, and improves accessibility for all users. The website at www.carrboronc.gov just underwent a redesign process by CivicPlus, the Town’s government website provider. The website now has improved functionality as well as a cleaner, easier layout to navigate. In addition to the primary government website, the Town of Carrboro offers an engagement platform at https://engage.carrboronc.gov/ ; “A modern, mobile-friendly, and intuitive site enables faster access to services, reduces administrative costs, and meets the high expectations of our residents for online interaction,” said Town Manager Patrice Toney. “Our efforts at modernizing online platforms are essential for fostering trust, ensuring legal compliance, and improving operational efficiency.” Residents and their local government will be able to access more information and more easily through some of the new featured modules: Improved calendar function that makes it easy to toggle between meetings and events Government area now features links to Town Pillars: Race and Equity and Climate Action Streamlines commonly accessed links under main categories including a new Projects area to provide status updates on town initiatives and improvements underway Provides prominent Translation function Includes a regular video feature Adds social media content to the homepage Complies with ADA standards for accessibility with more features to be added soon With the new website, the Town of Carrboro will increase its community engagement and enhance the communication between residents and local government. The website redesign was led by the Communication and Engagement Department. working closely with the Town Communications Team with representatives from across all departments and service areas. For more information or to offer feedback on the redesign, please contact us at communications@carrboronc.gov Carrboro Town News Posted on February 03, 2026

In addition to the primary government website, the Town of Carrboro offers an engagement platform at https://engage.carrboronc.gov/ ; “A modern, mobile-friendly, and intuitive site enables faster access to services, reduces administrative costs, and meets the high expectations of our residents for online interaction,” said Town Manager Patrice Toney. “Our efforts at modernizing online platforms are essential for fostering trust, ensuring legal compliance, and improving operational efficiency.” Residents and their local government will be able to access more information and more easily through some of the new featured modules: Improved calendar function that makes it easy to toggle between meetings and events Government area now features links to Town Pillars: Race and Equity and Climate Action Streamlines commonly accessed links under main categories including a new Projects area to provide status updates on town initiatives and improvements underway Provides prominent Translation function Includes a regular video feature Adds social media content to the homepage Complies with ADA standards for accessibility with more features to be added soon With the new website, the Town of Carrboro will increase its community engagement and enhance the communication between residents and local government. The website redesign was led by the Communication and Engagement Department. working closely with the Town Communications Team with representatives from across all departments and service areas. For more information or to offer feedback on the redesign, please contact us at communications@carrboronc.gov

CARRBORO, NC – The Town of Carrboro is pleased to announce that it has launched a website redesign that streamlines the homepage with commonly accessed pages, useable and relevant features, and improves accessibility for all users.
Residents and their local government will be able to access more information and more easily through some of the new featured modules:
Improved calendar function that makes it easy to toggle between meetings and events
Government area now features links to Town Pillars: Race and Equity and Climate Action
Streamlines commonly accessed links under main categories including a new Projects area to provide status updates on town initiatives and improvements underway
Adds social media content to the homepage
Complies with ADA standards for accessibility with more features to be added soon
With the new website, the Town of Carrboro will increase its community engagement and enhance the communication between residents and local government.
The website redesign was led by the Communication and Engagement Department. working closely with the Town Communications Team with representatives from across all departments and service areas.

CARRBORO, NC – Town Manager Patrice Toney has announced the selection of Marty Kornegay as the new information technology director for the Town of Carrboro. A former senior information technology director for a global healthcare company, Kornegay has more than two decades of experience in IT leadership across both public and private sectors.

A native of Kinston, N.C., Kornegay holds a Master of Science in Computer Science degree with a focus on Information Technology from Winston-Salem State University and a Bachelor of Arts in English from the University of North Carolina at Greensboro.

With more than 1,900 local governments participating in the GFOA Budget Awards Program across North America, Carrboro’s recognition places it among a select group of governments that have demonstrated excellence in budget presentation and financial communication.

The award highlights Carrboro’s ongoing efforts to strengthen fiscal accountability, improve public understanding of Town operations, and ensure that financial decisions reflect community values and strategic priorities.
GFOA award recipients and their budget documents are posted quarterly on the association’s website and serve as national models for other governments seeking to improve budgeting practices.

The Town of Carrboro is pleased to announce that a special celebration of Ms. Cotten is planned for the opening concert of the Freight Train Blues Concert Series on May 15, 2026. The concert series is produced by the Music Maker Foundation with support from the Town of Carrboro. The free evening concerts honor Cotten’s legacy in the world of roots music by emphasizing the cultural diversity, complexity, and vitality of her music and the music of many other artists local to her community and all over the country.
We encourage everyone to give her songs a listen!

Elizabeth Cotten Mural – This mural is featured on a building at 111 North Merritt Mill Road in Carrboro (on the border with Chapel Hill). It is a part of an ongoing series called the “North Carolina Musicians Murals Project,” which highlights famous musicians from North Carolina in their respective birthplaces. The mural was co-commissioned by the towns of Carrboro and Chapel Hill, located at the Cut Above Barber Shop located in the Midway, a historically Black business district that connects the towns. It is adjacent to the St. Paul African Methodist Episcopal Church.

About the Poet Laureate for Carrboro

A team of the local poetry community, including members of the Carrboro Poets Council and current and past local poets laureate, reviewed applications and conducted interviews with finalists.  The recommendation was made to the Recreation, Parks & Cultural Resources Advisory Board and approved at its December meeting.

The bus wrap was produced by the Town of Carrboro Communication and Engagement Department with design services by Carrboro-based The Splinter Group, which used striking local event imagery by photographer Jonathan Drake.

Many of the town’s community partners vital to programming these events joined us at the holiday parade – Liza Wolff-Francis, our wonderful outgoing poet laureate who has put on the West End Poetry Festival, Bryan Reklis who is gearing up for the Carrboro Film Fest in January, and Mauricio Solano of El Centro Hispano who helped put on Fiestas en la Calle: Festival LatinoAmericano as well as partner with the town on the Carrboro Orgullo Pride Celebration. In addition, the parade troupe featured Josephus Thompson III, the poet laureate for the City of Greensboro who was a featured presenter at a past West End Poetry Festival; and Rayah Charles, a singer who performed at the 2024 Juneteenth Celebration.
Carrboro Festivals featured on the bus wrap:
Who is featured on the Carrboro bus?

Vianna Fornville – Vianna is a prominent young activist and student leader at Chapel Hill High School and in her community. From her work with Blue Ribbon Mentor Advocate, the North Carolina NAACP Youth and College Division, and the James Cates Scholars, to being a Varsity Cheerleading Captain at CHHS, Vianna enjoys being at events that engage her community!
Jeremy Boomhower – A musician who plays in the Boom Unit Brass Band, he performed at the 2025 Carrboro Music Festival. Shown on the bus playing his sousaphone.

Hugh Swaso – Hugh Swaso is a Triangle-based session guitarist, entertainer, and fashion icon whose career includes extensive touring and international music work with a wide range of artists across genres. Having lived in the Triangle area for over 20 years, Swaso has become a familiar and influential presence in the local music community, known for both his musical versatility and distinctive stage style. Depicted here with the All Y’all Country All Stars—a local collective of the Triangle’s best musicians performing as a country band—he continues to influence fellow artists and captivate audiences at home and abroad. He performed in the 2025 Carrboro Music Festival.

Victoria "Tori" Roy - The front woman of WINFIELD, a Chapel Hill based vintage indie-rock band now together for 10 years with all original members, including guitarist Brad Prenda, bassist Kent Corley, and drummer David Bunch. WINFIELD performed at the 2025 Carrboro Music Festival and continues to perform regularly in the Triangle. Tori is a native of Hillsborough, a local live music appreciator, and has band rehearsals in her house in which she grew up, in the same spot that her mother had her own band rehearsals. She and her bandmates are honored to be included in the 2025 Carrboro Music Festival and especially on the new bus design alongside artists who bring so much magic to our community.

Oscar Garcia – An active Zumba instructor in Carrboro, Oscar is known for leading free, bilingual (English and Spanish) dance classes at the Carrboro Town Commons. He also participates in Carrboro in Motion. After being diagnosed with diabetes during the pandemic, he used Zumba to improve his own health and now shares his journey and promotes healthy living through his classes.
