Scam Alert: New Email Scam Poses as Town Asking for Fees Take precautions to avoid scams Post Date: August 11, 2025 4:00 p.m. A new email scam poses as the Town of Hillsborough and asks for payment. Updated Nov. 6: This version includes a link to the FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center for reporting the scam email. The Town of Hillsborough is aware of a new email scam that requests payment for permit fees. You can avoid being scammed by: Checking with the town or other government agency that is supposedly asking you to pay for a permit or other fees. Not replying to the email, which often requests that you reply to receive instructions on how to pay. The new scam appears to be sent from an email that ends in “plannings.us” or “hillsboroughnc.gov@usa.com" and may reference a real, past or present home improvement project at your address. The email asks for fees on behalf of the town’s planning and economic development division. Town emails end in “hillsboroughnc.gov.” News releases and bid postings sent by email come from townofhillsborough@public.govdelivery.com. If you receive an email that seems to be from the Town of Hillsborough and you would like to check on its validity, contact a town employee at an email address or phone number that you have previously used or through the town’s Contact Us page. Or call the town’s general number at 919-732-1270. Four Signs It’s a Scam The Federal Trade Commission notes scammers typically: Pretend to be from an organization you know, even showing up on your phone with a spoofed caller identification. Say there’s a problem with your account or a prize. Pressure you to act immediately. Tell you to pay in a specific way. Report Scams The Hillsborough Police Department asks those who receive this scam email to report it to the FBI’s Internet Crime Complaint Center . Note that there is a scam alert on the center’s website about scammers impersonating the Internet Crime Complaint Center. The center will not directly contact someone who files a complaint asking for information or money. In addition to reporting the scam email to the FBI, it is advised that you follow this advice from the Federal Trade Commission: Report the scam to the organization or company that was imitated by calling a number you know is real. Report the scam to the commission at ReportFraud.ftc.gov . Report the scam to your state attorney general . Let other people know about the scam so they can avoid it, too. If you have paid a scammer, see the Federal Trade Commission’s What to Do If You Were Scammed page, which includes information on how you might get your money back. Improvements include non-structural changes like lighting, signs and removal of mature trees . Some work may be approved by staff as a minor work or ordinary maintenance and repair. Historic District Commission Zoning Compliance Permits Pay Online Pay by Other Methods Fees and Charges Schedule Historic District Design Standards It is unlawful to begin construction, move, demolish, alter or restore any structure or site within the Historic District before one of the following: A Certificate of Appropriateness has been issued by the Historic District Commission. The project has been approved as a minor work or as ordinary maintenance by the Planning and Economic Development Division. Applicants should consult the design standards before performing improvements in the Historic District. Design Standards About the Design Standards The Historic District Design Standards were developed to guide the Historic District Commission in reviewing applications for: Changes to existing buildings or the historic district setting New construction and additions Relocation Demolition The standards also provide information on e xempt and minor works and o ther architectural and historic resources. Additional Resources The Historic District Commission also uses the matrices below to guide its decisions on applications. Compatibility Matrix Historic District Signage Matrix Certificate of Appropriateness Applicants are strongly encouraged to have a pre-application conference with town staff at least four to six weeks before the submittal deadline to discuss the project in detail. Apply for Certificate Application Examples The application packets below provide guidance on the information sought for a completed Certificate of Appropriateness application. The examples are not all-inclusive. Each application may have its own requirements based on the diversity and intensity of proposed changes. Planning staff encourage applicants to ask questions before submitting an application. Commercial renovation application example Fence application example Freestanding sign application example Porch renovation application example Residential addition application example Residential new construction application example Residential renovation application example Shed application example Wall and hanging sign application example Deadlines and Process Completed applications for a Certificate of Appropriateness must be submitted by noon Wednesday three weeks before a Historic District Commission meeting. For meeting information, see: Town Calendar Historic District Commission page Process for Review Applicants are encouraged to submit complete application packages as early as possible. The Historic District Commission allows a maximum of four major certificate applications on each agenda. Applications that are incomplete or received after the first four packages deemed complete by staff will be placed on the next agenda that has available space. Complete applications for work deemed to be minor but requiring commission review will be added to an agenda as space becomes available. Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Apply for Business License Contact Financial Services OUTDOOR SEATING PERMIT An outdoor seating permit allows businesses to serve customers at tables outside on the public sidewalk and in public space. These conditions apply: Tables must be placed directly next to the business’s storefront. Tables must belong to the business. Seating areas must be clearly delineated. Scam Alert An email scam is posing as the Town of Hillsborough and requests payment for permit fees. Read More The Planning and Economic Development Division issues zoning compliance permits for activities regulated under the Unified Development Ordinance that are requested on property in the town's limits and planning area. In some cases, a building permit may be required. The Orange County Planning and Inspections Department oversees building permits and inspections. Unified Development Ordinance Pay Online Pay by Other Methods Change of Use Permit Zoning compliance permits are issued for changes of use, such as moving a business into an existing non-residential retail or office space. Apply for Change of Use Permit Home Occupation Permit Before starting a home business, contact planning staff to describe the business you plan to open. Staff will help determine if the type of business you want to open meets conditions for a zoning compliance permit. The regulations guide and checklist below lists businesses prohibited from home occupation permits. Home Occupation FAQs Starting a Home Occupation Local Regulations Guide and Checklist Apply for Home Occupation Permit New Construction and Additions Permit Zoning compliance permits are issued for site changes, such as relocating or expanding an existing structure or constructing a new building, accessory building, deck or fence. See a sample plot plan . Apply for Permit Signs Permit Zoning compliance permits are issued before installing new signs or changing the location, sign face or message of an existing sign. Apply for Signs Permit Additional Information Accessory Dwelling Units Accessory dwelling units are secondary dwellings added to the property of a main dwelling. A dwelling unit must contain cooking, sleeping and bathing facilities. A kitchen must have cooking equipment and a sink. A bathroom must have a sink, toilet and shower or bathtub. Some neighborhoods have covenants that restrict multiple units. Property owners are responsible for checking with their homeowners association before applying for permits. Types of Accessory Units The town has defined two types of accessory units. Freestanding Units These include garage apartments or "granny flats." The parcel must be zoned Residential 10, 15 or 20 (R-10, R-15 or R-20). These designations are common to neighborhoods built before the town implemented special use zoning around the year 2000. Neighborhoods in special use zoning districts are not able to build freestanding units. They are built to higher densities and often don’t have lots large enough to accommodate a freestanding unit. These neighborhoods include Collins Ridge, Fiori Hill, Forest Ridge and Waterstone Estates. In-home Units These are fully attached to the main dwelling, such as an efficiency or basement apartment. The accessory unit must have its own exterior access. If interior access to the principal dwelling is provided, it must be lockable from both dwellings. Difference Between Zoning and Building Permits Building permits deal with compliance with the state building code for structural, electrical, mechanical and plumbing work. Orange County issues these permits and enforces these codes. The Town of Hillsborough issues zoning permits to verify that the use of a structure is allowed in a given district and that structures are not too tall or too close to the property line. Any project that changes the footprint of a structure or adds a new structure or fence requires a zoning permit if the lot is within the town’s planning jurisdiction. Interactive Zoning Map For detailed zoning information, use the town's interactive zoning map. See the guide for instructions. Use Interactive Zoning Map Submitting an Application Applications can be submitted: By email to planning@hillsboroughnc.gov. In person at Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. By mail to Planning and Economic Development Division, PO Box 429, Hillsborough, NC 27278. Staff typically process permit applications in one to two business days. Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9470 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Planning Staff Title Phone Email Boyle, Molly Senior Planner 919-296-9473 Email Kimbrough, Dakotah Planning Technician 919-296-9470 Email

Accessory Dwelling Units Accessory dwelling units are secondary dwellings added to the property of a main dwelling. A dwelling unit must contain cooking, sleeping and bathing facilities. A kitchen must have cooking equipment and a sink. A bathroom must have a sink, toilet and shower or bathtub. Some neighborhoods have covenants that restrict multiple units. Property owners are responsible for checking with their homeowners association before applying for permits. Types of Accessory Units The town has defined two types of accessory units. Freestanding Units These include garage apartments or "granny flats." The parcel must be zoned Residential 10, 15 or 20 (R-10, R-15 or R-20). These designations are common to neighborhoods built before the town implemented special use zoning around the year 2000. Neighborhoods in special use zoning districts are not able to build freestanding units. They are built to higher densities and often don’t have lots large enough to accommodate a freestanding unit. These neighborhoods include Collins Ridge, Fiori Hill, Forest Ridge and Waterstone Estates. In-home Units These are fully attached to the main dwelling, such as an efficiency or basement apartment. The accessory unit must have its own exterior access. If interior access to the principal dwelling is provided, it must be lockable from both dwellings. Difference Between Zoning and Building Permits Building permits deal with compliance with the state building code for structural, electrical, mechanical and plumbing work. Orange County issues these permits and enforces these codes. The Town of Hillsborough issues zoning permits to verify that the use of a structure is allowed in a given district and that structures are not too tall or too close to the property line. Any project that changes the footprint of a structure or adds a new structure or fence requires a zoning permit if the lot is within the town’s planning jurisdiction. Interactive Zoning Map For detailed zoning information, use the town's interactive zoning map. See the guide for instructions. Use Interactive Zoning Map Submitting an Application Applications can be submitted: By email to planning@hillsboroughnc.gov. In person at Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. By mail to Planning and Economic Development Division, PO Box 429, Hillsborough, NC 27278. Staff typically process permit applications in one to two business days.

Pay Online Pay by Other Methods Fees and Charges Schedule Special Event Permit Determining a Special Event A special event permit is required for most events that meet at least one of the following: Are open to the general public. Are over 6 hours long. Require street closings. Requesting permission for a prohibited activity, such as archery, sound amplifying equipment, and temporary chalk marking associated with sports and games. See Town Park Use and Event Management in Code of Ordinances. Public Gatherings The Town of Hillsborough fully supports people's rights to stand up for their beliefs and values. Free speech is protected for everyone under the First Amendment of the U.S. Constitution and is critical to our democracy. Anyone inviting the public to participate in an event or to gather in public spaces should apply for a special event permit. The permit helps the town plan for appropriate staffing to ensure public safety at public gatherings. Public gatherings include demonstrations, marches, protests, rallies and vigils. Permit Conditions The town expects people demonstrating their First Amendment rights to conduct themselves in a civil manner and to avoid escalating confrontation by actions such as using profanity, threatening violence, or approaching any opposing groups. Below are conditions for the permit: Unimpeded travel — Participants cannot obstruct, accost or maliciously detain pedestrians or block a pedestrian's path or do the same for vehicular traffic on adjacent roadways. Crowd size — If the event becomes too large to reasonably gather on the Orange County Historic Courthouse lawns or near sidewalks without impacting pedestrian or vehicular travel, the event participants will need to spread out, disperse or move to a larger public gathering space. If organizers do not disperse participants, law enforcement may ask participants to disperse. Law enforcement guidance — At all times, organizers and participants should obey the direction and safety measures prescribed by officials with the Hillsborough Police Department and Orange County Sheriff's Office. Permit Application and Process Application Process Permit applications must be received at least 60 days in advance of the requested event. To apply, complete the application and include a map with the event's location marked. Applicants for events on the Riverwalk greenway should understand that the greenway would remain open to the public. Gold Park, Kings Highway Park and the Riverwalk greenway are closed for repairs due to damage from Tropical Storm Chantal in July. Parts of Riverwalk may open before the parks. Check with staff on estimated openings. Apply for Special Event Permit Applications can be submitted by: Email — dakotah.kimbrough@hillsboroughnc.gov In person — Town Hall, 101 E. Place up to five temporary signs around town. Apply for Signs Permit Contact Economic Development Permit Fees Fees are: Banner-only reservation and permit — $5. Banner reservation and up to five additional signs permit — $20. Reservations Reserve a park facility or the town's special event banner location on Churton Street. Riparian Buffers The town enforces a 50-foot vegetated riparian (stream) buffer along streams and the Eno River to: Reduce erosion. Encourage runoff to soak into the ground. Protect water quality. Use or Impact Must Be Approved Riparian buffers should remain undisturbed if possible. Clearing and building within a stream buffer requires approval. Allowable activities are typically minor in impact but require submitting and receiving approval of an analysis showing that no practical alternative exists. Typically allowed are: Impact from roadways, driveways and utilities. Agricultural and forestry activities within a buffer if state guidelines are followed. Resources Hillsborough Unified Development Ordinance Section 6.20.17 . The town’s riparian buffer rules contain a list of uses categorized as allowable or allowable with mitigation. North Carolina Neuse River Basin Buffer Rule through the Riparian Buffer Protection Program page of the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. Riparian (Stream) Buffers

The town regulates connections to its water supply to prevent contamination and to protect public safety. Backflow prevention devices ensure water flows only in one direction. A number of uses require installation of a backflow prevention device, including irrigation systems. A backflow prevention device with protection for the winter Tampering and Theft About Backflow What is it? Backflow is the unwanted reversal of the flow of water and substances. Typically, water will flow from the public water supply to the consumer’s plumbing system. When backflow occurs, the water flows from the non-drinking water source to the drinking water source and potentially contaminates it. What causes it? Backflow incidents are caused by one of the following: Back pressure — This is created when the pressure within the customer’s system becomes greater than the water supply pressure. Elevated tanks, heating systems and booster pumps are some of the main causes of back pressure. Back siphonage — This is created when the water supply main has negative or reduced pressure. Water main breaks or hydrant flushing are two of the main causes of back siphonage. About How the Town Protects Water The town has requirements to protect the public drinking water supply. Containment Protection — The town installs backflow prevention at the service meter to protect the public water supply from contamination. Isolation Protection — The town requires backflow prevention at the fixture where water is used. This protects the customer's internal drinking water plumbing from contamination. Uses Requiring Backflow Prevention Several types of facilities are required to install a backflow prevention device. If your property or business type is not included in the list below and you have a specific question about your legal requirements, contact the town's utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. Auto repair shops Auxiliary water supply (wells) Barber shops and beauty salons Breweries Buildings that reuse or recycle water Buildings with commercial or public kitchens, groundwater wells, multiple water service lines, roof tanks and elevated storage lines, or water-cooled equipment or chillers Canneries Commercial car washes Dye plants Exterminators Fire sprinkler system Food preparation facilities Food processing plants and meat or fish packers Funeral parlors Greenhouses Lawn irrigation systems Metal plating, cleaning, processing or fabricating facilities Hospitals, clinics and laboratories (including veterinary hospitals) Ice manufacturing facilities Large residential dwellings with water boilers that use rust-inhibitors or other water treatment chemicals (treated water boilers) Laundries and dry cleaners Medical and dental offices Morgues, mortuaries and autopsy facilities Nursing homes Paper processors Photo-processing facilities Printing facilities Schools and colleges Sewage treatment plants or handling facilities Slaughterhouses and live poultry processing facilities Swimming pools Supermarkets Tanneries Warehouses with toxic chemical storage Cross-Connection Control Requirements See Cross-Connection Control Policy . No Direct Connections The town prohibits direct connections between the water supply and any potential source of contamination. Potential sources of contamination must be isolated by an air gap or a reduced pressure zone backflow assembly. A direct connection would occur if a garden hose became submerged in a swimming pool, hot tub, soapy water bucket, pond or pet water dish. Water Use by Businesses Contracted businesses working in Hillsborough and needing water must do one of the following: Transport water into town. Purchase bulk water from the town. Rent a hydrant meter from the town. The orange box that is connected to some hydrants contains a meter and a backflow prevention device. See: Start or Stop Service page for bulk water purchases or hydrant meter rentals Tampering and Theft page Outdoor Faucet Requirements The town requires a hose bib vacuum breaker on every outside faucet. The vacuum breaker must be certified by the NSF 61 standard. The American Water Works Association recommends replacing vacuum breakers every five years or if they are failing. The device has failed if water is spraying out of the breaker's weep holes. Testing The town contracts with Backflow Solutions to manage its backflow test data. Customers using backflow prevention assemblies must: Have the assemblies tested when installed. Maintain their assemblies and h ave them tested annually by qualified individuals. Submit results of testing and maintenance to Backflow Solutions. The company maintains a list of licensed, registered testing agencies on its website. Contact Backflow Solutions at 800-414-4990 or bsionline@backflow.com. Visit its website at BSI Online . Process Existing Assemblies — Licensed testers must submit test results through the Backflow Solutions website . Failed Test — T he assembly must be repaired within 10 days if it fails a test. A certified tester must submit retest results on the Backflow Solutions website . New Installations — A licensed tester who performed the work must sign a printed test form. A copy must be emailed to Backflow Solutions. Preventing Backflow Backflow Prevention Methods Air Gap — An air gap is the vertical space between a water inlet and the rim of a receptacle receiving public water. Examples are a faucet or hose providing water to a bucket, sink or pool. The air gap must be at least twice the diameter of the water inlet and no smaller than 1 inch. Backflow Assembly — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet and outlet shutoff valves. It has four test cocks, making inline testing possible. Public water systems, which regulate connections to the public water supply, generally require backflow assemblies rather than devices. Backflow Device — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet or outlet shutoff valves and usually does not have test cocks. Inline testing is not possible for devices without test cocks. The devices are not approved by the University of Southern California Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research. Backflow devices generally are governed by plumbing code when a building is constructed or upfitted. Elimination — Eliminating the cross-connection is necessary when the hazard has been improperly installed and plumbed or when the hazard is no longer in use. Common Backflow Prevention Assemblies and Uses Double Check Valve Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler system with no booster pumps, most commercial establishments Double Check Valve Detector Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as non-health hazards Reduced Pressure Detector Assembly Health and non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as health hazards Reduced Pressure Principal Assembly (preferred assembly) Health and non-health hazards Hospitals, car washes, lawn irrigation, mortuaries Common Backflow Prevention Devices and Uses Pressure Vacuum Breaker Back siphonage only Multi-zone irrigation systems Hose Connection Vacuum Breaker (not testable) Back siphonage only Garden hose connections Protecting Devices in Winter Protection All backflow devices are required to be in a class 1 or 2 enclosure that meets the American Society of Sanitary Engineering’s 1060 standard. These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information. Recommendations for Pool and Hot Tub Owners Due to the risk for backflow, the town recommends that customers filling swimming pools, hot tubs, ponds or other bodies of water: Use the air gap method. See the section on backflow prevention methods. Avoid times when hydrants are opened for flushing. This occurs in March and occasionally in the fall. Water System Flushing Lower Water Pressure — When hydrants are flushed, pressure in the water system drops. Water could backflow into the public water supply system if water main pressure is lower and a hose becomes submerged in a body of water being filled. Chemical Change — During the annual March flushing, the town’s water is disinfected by chlorination instead of chloramines (ammonia and chlorine). The state requires this change in disinfection for one month each year. The change could upset the balance of a pool, pond or spa. Contact Information Adron F. About Backflow What is it? Backflow is the unwanted reversal of the flow of water and substances. Typically, water will flow from the public water supply to the consumer’s plumbing system. When backflow occurs, the water flows from the non-drinking water source to the drinking water source and potentially contaminates it. What causes it? Backflow incidents are caused by one of the following: Back pressure — This is created when the pressure within the customer’s system becomes greater than the water supply pressure. Elevated tanks, heating systems and booster pumps are some of the main causes of back pressure. Back siphonage — This is created when the water supply main has negative or reduced pressure. Water main breaks or hydrant flushing are two of the main causes of back siphonage. About How the Town Protects Water The town has requirements to protect the public drinking water supply. Containment Protection — The town installs backflow prevention at the service meter to protect the public water supply from contamination. Isolation Protection — The town requires backflow prevention at the fixture where water is used. This protects the customer's internal drinking water plumbing from contamination. Uses Requiring Backflow Prevention Several types of facilities are required to install a backflow prevention device. If your property or business type is not included in the list below and you have a specific question about your legal requirements, contact the town's utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. Auto repair shops Auxiliary water supply (wells) Barber shops and beauty salons Breweries Buildings that reuse or recycle water Buildings with commercial or public kitchens, groundwater wells, multiple water service lines, roof tanks and elevated storage lines, or water-cooled equipment or chillers Canneries Commercial car washes Dye plants Exterminators Fire sprinkler system Food preparation facilities Food processing plants and meat or fish packers Funeral parlors Greenhouses Lawn irrigation systems Metal plating, cleaning, processing or fabricating facilities Hospitals, clinics and laboratories (including veterinary hospitals) Ice manufacturing facilities Large residential dwellings with water boilers that use rust-inhibitors or other water treatment chemicals (treated water boilers) Laundries and dry cleaners Medical and dental offices Morgues, mortuaries and autopsy facilities Nursing homes Paper processors Photo-processing facilities Printing facilities Schools and colleges Sewage treatment plants or handling facilities Slaughterhouses and live poultry processing facilities Swimming pools Supermarkets Tanneries Warehouses with toxic chemical storage

No Direct Connections The town prohibits direct connections between the water supply and any potential source of contamination. Potential sources of contamination must be isolated by an air gap or a reduced pressure zone backflow assembly. A direct connection would occur if a garden hose became submerged in a swimming pool, hot tub, soapy water bucket, pond or pet water dish. Water Use by Businesses Contracted businesses working in Hillsborough and needing water must do one of the following: Transport water into town. Purchase bulk water from the town. Rent a hydrant meter from the town. The orange box that is connected to some hydrants contains a meter and a backflow prevention device. See: Start or Stop Service page for bulk water purchases or hydrant meter rentals Tampering and Theft page Outdoor Faucet Requirements The town requires a hose bib vacuum breaker on every outside faucet. The vacuum breaker must be certified by the NSF 61 standard. The American Water Works Association recommends replacing vacuum breakers every five years or if they are failing. The device has failed if water is spraying out of the breaker's weep holes. Testing The town contracts with Backflow Solutions to manage its backflow test data. Customers using backflow prevention assemblies must: Have the assemblies tested when installed. Maintain their assemblies and h ave them tested annually by qualified individuals. Submit results of testing and maintenance to Backflow Solutions. The company maintains a list of licensed, registered testing agencies on its website. Contact Backflow Solutions at 800-414-4990 or bsionline@backflow.com. Visit its website at BSI Online . Process Existing Assemblies — Licensed testers must submit test results through the Backflow Solutions website . Failed Test — T he assembly must be repaired within 10 days if it fails a test. A certified tester must submit retest results on the Backflow Solutions website . New Installations — A licensed tester who performed the work must sign a printed test form. A copy must be emailed to Backflow Solutions.

Backflow Prevention Methods Air Gap — An air gap is the vertical space between a water inlet and the rim of a receptacle receiving public water. Examples are a faucet or hose providing water to a bucket, sink or pool. The air gap must be at least twice the diameter of the water inlet and no smaller than 1 inch. Backflow Assembly — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet and outlet shutoff valves. It has four test cocks, making inline testing possible. Public water systems, which regulate connections to the public water supply, generally require backflow assemblies rather than devices. Backflow Device — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet or outlet shutoff valves and usually does not have test cocks. Inline testing is not possible for devices without test cocks. The devices are not approved by the University of Southern California Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research. Backflow devices generally are governed by plumbing code when a building is constructed or upfitted. Elimination — Eliminating the cross-connection is necessary when the hazard has been improperly installed and plumbed or when the hazard is no longer in use. Common Backflow Prevention Assemblies and Uses Double Check Valve Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler system with no booster pumps, most commercial establishments Double Check Valve Detector Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as non-health hazards Reduced Pressure Detector Assembly Health and non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as health hazards Reduced Pressure Principal Assembly (preferred assembly) Health and non-health hazards Hospitals, car washes, lawn irrigation, mortuaries Common Backflow Prevention Devices and Uses Pressure Vacuum Breaker Back siphonage only Multi-zone irrigation systems Hose Connection Vacuum Breaker (not testable) Back siphonage only Garden hose connections Protecting Devices in Winter Protection All backflow devices are required to be in a class 1 or 2 enclosure that meets the American Society of Sanitary Engineering’s 1060 standard. These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information. Recommendations for Pool and Hot Tub Owners Due to the risk for backflow, the town recommends that customers filling swimming pools, hot tubs, ponds or other bodies of water: Use the air gap method. See the section on backflow prevention methods. Avoid times when hydrants are opened for flushing. This occurs in March and occasionally in the fall. Water System Flushing Lower Water Pressure — When hydrants are flushed, pressure in the water system drops. Water could backflow into the public water supply system if water main pressure is lower and a hose becomes submerged in a body of water being filled. Chemical Change — During the annual March flushing, the town’s water is disinfected by chlorination instead of chloramines (ammonia and chlorine). The state requires this change in disinfection for one month each year. The change could upset the balance of a pool, pond or spa.

General information Reservations must be requested at least 14 days before the planned event date. Requests that extend past a park’s closing time will be denied. No electricity is offered with reservations. Outlets at parks are low amperage, only powering small devices like phones. Bounce houses are not permitted. Reservations are posted weekly at the parks. Direct anyone in the area you reserved to the bulletin or site. If issues arise during your reservation, call 911 for assistance. Hillsborough police officers are dispatched through 911. Reservation Conditions Reservations allow exclusive rights only to the particular area requested. Proposed activity or use of the park must not: Unreasonably interfere or detract from the general public’s enjoyment of the park or from the promotion of public health, welfare, safety and recreation. Be anticipated to incite violence, crime or disorderly conduct. Involve unusual, extraordinary or burdensome expense or police operation by the town. Events are limited to 75 people.

Cancellations made prior to seven days before the reservation date may be credited the amount paid, with credits usable for one year.

Be anticipated to incite violence, crime or disorderly conduct. Involve unusual, extraordinary or burdensome expense or police operation by the town. Events are limited to 75 people.

General Information Reservations must be requested at least 14 days before the planned event date. Requests that extend past a park’s closing time will be denied. No electricity is offered with reservations. Outlets at parks are low amperage, only powering small devices like phones. Bounce houses are not permitted. Reservations are posted weekly at the parks. Direct anyone in the area you reserved to the bulletin or site. If issues arise during your reservation, call 911 for assistance. Hillsborough police officers are dispatched through 911.
The Gold Park large picnic shelter may be reserved.
The small shelter adjacent to restrooms is not reservable.

Location — Intersection of South Churton and Mayo streets The town’s location for special event banners is reservable for up to two weeks for public events in Hillsborough or its planning jurisdiction. The banner location is part of the “Welcome to Hillsborough” sign. Fees The fees for the special event banner are: Banner-only reservation and permit — $5. Banner reservation and up to five additional signs permit — $20. Fees and Charges Schedule Requirements Special event banners and signage require a zoning compliance permit from the town. In addition to the reservable banner location, up to five signs may be placed around town with the permit. General Information Reservation and permit applications must be submitted at least three business days before signage is placed. Town staff will remove any banners or event signs improperly located, exceeding size limits or left beyond the permitted time. Requirements for Banners The only approved location for banners is between the flagpoles that are part of the “Welcome to Hillsborough” sign at the intersection of South Churton and Mayo streets. Banners may be displayed for two weeks. They may advertise community activities, functions, festivals and similar events open to the public. Functions of a political or for-profit nature are not permitted. Banners must be: Made of a mesh material or have air vents every 3 feet. Tied to the banner supports using one-quarter-inch nylon rope at each corner. Flagpoles cannot be used. About 30 inches tall, with a maximum height of 36 inches and a maximum length of 15 feet. The banner supports are spaced about 18 feet apart. Requirements for Additional Signs No event may display more than five additional signs. Total signage is limited to 100 square feet. On Site — One sign no larger than 32 square feet may be on the lot hosting the event for the same display period. The sign may be a banner if it is securely fastened and adequately vented to pose no traffic threat. Off Site — Four signs may be displayed off site in locations noted in the permit application. They may be placed in the public right of way but not in a driveway or intersection sight triangle. The signs must be rigid (not banners), and a single side may not exceed 8 square feet. Signs may be erected 14 days before the event and must be removed within 48 hours after the event. This allows for a maximum display of 17 days. Reserve the Banner See Available Times Reserve Special Event Banner Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9470 Office Hours — 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Reservations Administrator

Department Functions Water and Sewer Billing and Collections The Financial Services Department handles billing and collections for water and sewer service, including meter reading and starting and stopping service. It also maintains the Hillsborough Water Assistance Program in partnership with the nonprofit organization Orange Congregations in Mission, which administers the program. Other Functions The department also: Issues business licenses. Collects permit fees, prepared food and beverage tax funds and room occupancy tax funds. Handles accounts receivable and payable, purchase orders and requisitions. Maintains insurance verifications, town contracts and the town's capital asset records. Maintains the town's payments and donations site.

The Financial Services Department: Starts, stops and transfers billing accounts. Reads and maintains water meters. Performs service disconnections. Collects monthly payments. Emergencies and Alerts Give or Receive Aid Pay Online or By Phone Rates Start or Stop Service Tampering and Theft Fees and Charges Schedule Bill Adjustments Call customer service at 919-296-9450 for assistance. Bill adjustments will not be made if a meter or service connection has been tampered with. Leak Adjustment Customers may request a bill adjustment if a water line break has resulted in significantly greater charges for a billing period. One of the following is required: A plumber or maintenance person must provide sufficient documentation of a leak. Customers must provide receipts for items purchased for repairs. Customers must request leak adjustments no later than two months after the billing date for the adjustment. Customers are allowed one leak adjustment in a 12-month period. If an adjustment is approved, the customer will be charged an amount based on an average of the previous six months. Leak Adjustment Request Leak Adjustment Request (Español) Malfunctioning Water Meter If a meter fails to register water use, the customer will be charged an amount for water and sewer usage based on an average of the previous six months. The standard for meter accuracy is plus or minus 10%. A meter flow test can be requested, with a fee charged if the meter passes the flow test. Contact Customer Service Billing Water and sewer usage are billed monthly. Customer Information Ensure your customer information is up to date. Please update your information with the town whenever you have a change in: Billing address Email address Telephone number Customer Information Update Due Date Your bill should arrive by mail during the first week of the month. Your payment due date for current charges is the 25th day of each month, with payment due the following business day if the due date falls on a weekend or holiday. Payments received after 11:59 p.m. on the due date are late. Meter Readings The town has about 7,000 water meters in its service area and two employees responsible for the meters. They: Read, repair, replace and test meters. Connect and disconnect water service. Meter information is gathered electronically from the employees' vehicles on a monthly basis. The employees will physically check meters that produce unusual readings, such as high, low or no usage. Service Disconnection Service is disconnected one month after payment has not been received (the previous month’s bill must be paid by the current month’s due date). To have service reconnected, pay the full balance and all penalties and fees. Fees and Charges Schedule How to Pay Bills The town encourages paying online or by telephone at 833-262-5902 with credit or debit card. Both options are available 24 hours a day. Bank Draft Have the town automatically draft payment from your bank account on the 10th or 25th day of each month. Submit the form with a voided check by mail, in person or by deposit box. Pay by other methods until your bill states, “Do not pay. Paid by draft.” Enrollment takes at least one billing cycle. Starting electronic payments from your bank can take up to 90 days. Your bank will issue a paper check for payment during this setup period. Log on to your bank's online bill pay service and enter Town of Hillsborough as a payee with this “remit to” address: Town of Hillsborough, PO Box 429, Hillsborough, NC 27278. Enter your account or customer number in the format shown on your bill. Deposit Box, In Person or Mail Deposit Box — Leave non-cash payments in the box at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. In Person — Make cash or check payments or get staff help with credit or debit card payments between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m. weekdays at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Mail — Send payments to Town of Hillsborough, PO Box 429, Hillsborough, NC 27278. Online Payments Site Registration To use the online features, v isit the town payments and donations website to register. Account numbers are provided on bills. To confirm your registration on the website, you will need your account number and the amount you last paid for water or sewer charges. Contact customer service for the amount to enter if you do not have your last paid bill. Benefits Utility customers who register to use the online platform can: View utility bills online. Set up automatic payments from debit or credit cards. Sign up for text alerts. Register for bills by email. They also: Have access to their account information around the clock daily, including usage history and monthly readings. Have ability to print current and previous bills. Do not have to remember their last payment amount to make a payment. Can pay electronically, potentially saving time and avoiding late fees. Fees The town does not add any fees or surcharges to electronic payments of utility bills at this time. Automatic bank draft is a convenient payment method that generates no fees for customers and minimal fees for the town. Contact Information Town Hall Annex, 105 E. The Financial Services Department arranges service connections and disconnections, schedules meter readings, and collects monthly payments. Fees and Charges Schedule

Start Service Contact customer service at least three business days in advance of the requested start date. A $20 fee is charged to transfer accounts between addresses. To start service, the town requires all of the following: Connection fee of $20 (non-refundable) Deposit of $75 in town or $150 out of town Copy of one of the following signed by all parties: Deed, settlement statement or purchase agreement Lease agreement Property management agreement Social Security number Valid driver’s license, government ID or passport Starting Service Create an account on the town's online payment site to start water service. Learn more about the online payments site on the Billing and Adjustments page. Start Service Stop Service Submit a request to disconnect service at least three business days before the requested end date. Stop Service Sign in to the town's online payments site or sign up for an account to stop water service. Learn more about the online payments site on the Billing and Adjustments page. Stop Service

Bulk Water Contractors may obtain bulk water from the Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant, 711 Dimmocks Mill Road. Bulk water is charged at the out-of-town water rate. Pay first at the Financial Services Department in the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. The billing and collections office is open 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. How to Purchase Bulk Water Hydrant Use (Water Meter Rental) Contractors may purchase water from hydrants by renting 3-inch hydrant meters. The meters measure water usage for proper billing. The rental period must be at least two months. The meters are housed in orange boxes connected to the hydrants. The boxes contain a backflow preventer to ensure contaminants do not enter the public water system. Rent Hydrant Meter Irrigation Systems See information about irrigation requirements and limitations on the town's Water Conservation page. Plumbing Permit The North Carolina Plumbing Code requires a permit from the local inspections department for installation of an irrigation system. The Orange County Planning and Inspections Department issues permits and performs inspections. Contact the department at 919-245-2600. Fees and Other Charges The Town of Hillsborough charges a system development fee to cover the cost of water supplies, treatment facilities, pumps, piping and other infrastructure necessary to provide service to an irrigation-only meter. The fee is the same as that for a regular water service, but sewer fees are not charged. Irrigation-only water service is charged monthly per 1,000 gallons of water use. The town may halt irrigation-only service during water shortages, such as due to droughts. If the service is halted, the town will bill only for the actual amount of water used during the affected months. It will not charge a minimum fee during those months. Any changes to rates and fees will occur typically at the start of a new fiscal year, which is July 1. For current rates, see the town's Fees and Charges Schedule or contact utilities staff using the Utilities contact form . Supplemental Irrigation Permit Permits for supplemental irrigation may be purchased to protect new plantings for 90 days. To be eligible, a property must have an active building permit or have had one within the previous 90 days. No permits are issued during water restrictions at Stage 2 or higher. For permit prices, see the town's Fees and Charges Schedule .

Hydrant Use (Water Meter Rental) Contractors may purchase water from hydrants by renting 3-inch hydrant meters. The meters measure water usage for proper billing. The rental period must be at least two months. The meters are housed in orange boxes connected to the hydrants. The boxes contain a backflow preventer to ensure contaminants do not enter the public water system. Rent Hydrant Meter

The meters are housed in orange boxes connected to the hydrants. The boxes contain a backflow preventer to ensure contaminants do not enter the public water system. Rent Hydrant Meter
Contractors may purchase water from hydrants by renting 3-inch hydrant meters. The meters measure water usage for proper billing. The rental period must be at least two months.
The meters are housed in orange boxes connected to the hydrants. The boxes contain a backflow preventer to ensure contaminants do not enter the public water system.

Irrigation Systems See information about irrigation requirements and limitations on the town's Water Conservation page. Plumbing Permit The North Carolina Plumbing Code requires a permit from the local inspections department for installation of an irrigation system. The Orange County Planning and Inspections Department issues permits and performs inspections. Contact the department at 919-245-2600. Fees and Other Charges The Town of Hillsborough charges a system development fee to cover the cost of water supplies, treatment facilities, pumps, piping and other infrastructure necessary to provide service to an irrigation-only meter. The fee is the same as that for a regular water service, but sewer fees are not charged. Irrigation-only water service is charged monthly per 1,000 gallons of water use. The town may halt irrigation-only service during water shortages, such as due to droughts. If the service is halted, the town will bill only for the actual amount of water used during the affected months. It will not charge a minimum fee during those months. Any changes to rates and fees will occur typically at the start of a new fiscal year, which is July 1. For current rates, see the town's Fees and Charges Schedule or contact utilities staff using the Utilities contact form . For permit prices, see the town's Fees and Charges Schedule .

The Water Assistance Program provides short-term help to customers in paying their water and sewer bills when there is a proven need. It is funded through donations. About 800 to 900 accounts are overdue each month, and customers sometimes incur late fees and service disruptions. State law prevents charging different rates based on income.

Note the donation is for the Hillsborough Water Assistance Program.
Eligible candidates must be recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. For more information, contact:

Program Start The town started the program in 2006 to help customers in need pay water and sewer bills. It partnered with the local nonprofit organization Orange Congregations in Mission to manage the program. In both the first and second years of the program, the town provided $5,000 of seed money from its general fund.

Customers fund the operation of the town's water and sewer system, including the infrastructure needed to treat, transport and protect water. Please immediately call 911 to report any tampering or theft. See Fees and Charges Schedule for fees and penalties. See Utilities: Prohibited Activities in the Code of Ordinances. Water can be legally and safely obtained from a fire hydrant with an orange box. Billing and Adjustments Start or Stop Service Tampering and Theft of Infrastructure Backflow Prevention Devices The town own some backflow prevention devices on town properties. Most are privately owned. If you see anyone tampering with a backflow prevention device or have experienced a theft of the device: Call 911 to have an officer dispatched. Contact the utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. The devices are housed in utility boxes and prevent contaminated water from entering potable water in a building. They can be targets for theft due to their metal content. Property owners who have had a backflow preventer stolen or damaged will need to employ the services of a plumber. Preventing Theft Options for preventing theft include: Employing community or neighborhood watch prevention, asking community members to report any suspicious behavior to police. Adding a lock to the backflow preventer box or installing a locking box, available from a plumber or from a plumbing warehouse. If locks are added, access must be given to the town. Cutting back any shrubs or other plantings to allow some view of the device and ensuring that an area of at least 5 feet around the utility is free of any growth. Water Meters Any tampering of a meter or service connection will result in the meter being locked and a tampering fee and civil penalty. Service will not be reconnected until all fees are paid in full. Repeated tampering will result in removal of the meter, additional fees to reset and reactivate it, and potential civil or criminal charges. Theft of Water Safety and Operational Concerns Water theft can cause concerns for the public water system including: Water hammer (form of hydraulic shock) in the system that breaks pipes and damages equipment. Lowered water pressure for customers during the time of theft. Contamination of the public water system. Costs associated with repairing damage to the system. Theft from Hydrants If you see a person or business taking water from a water hydrant without an orange meter box, report it immediately to 911 and to the utility system superintendent. To help the town prosecute the case: Discreetly take a photo if possible. Note the time, date, location, vehicle name and number, and license plate. Legally Obtained Water Contractors are able to purchase bulk water from the town. They can: Obtain water from the Water Treatment Plant by paying in advance at the Town Hall Annex. Rent water meters to legally obtain water from a fire hydrant. The meters are housed in an orange box with a backflow prevention device to safely obtain water. See the Start or Stop Service page. Contact Information Adron F. Thompson Facility, 715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Office Hours — 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday. Emergencies Tampering or Theft — Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Staff from the Budget Division and Financial Services Department manage the budget and finances for the town. Two volunteer boards also have annual budgets to handle funds they oversee: the Tourism Board and Tourism Development Authority . Nonprofit Partnerships Stormwater Fees Tax Rates and Compliance Water and Sewer Rates Budget Budget Budget The budget is a financial plan for the fiscal year (July 1-June 30). Hillsborough uses a three‐year format to better see any fiscal or operational challenges.  The budget's first year is legally binding. Years 2 and 3 show needs to plan for. The budget contains: An estimate of proposed expenditures. The proposed means of financing the expenditures. Goals and objectives of the expenditures. The Budget Division is responsible for preparing the town's budget. When possible, the town applies for federal, state and other grants to cover some of the cost of town projects. Manager's Recommended Budget Fiscal Year 2027 Budget and Financial Plan Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Message Fiscal Year 2027 Budget at a Glance Current Budget Fiscal Year 2026 Budget Ordinance Fiscal Year 2026 Budget and Financial Plan Fiscal Year 2026 Budget at a Glance Fiscal Year 2026 Fees and Charges Schedule Fiscal Year 2026 Budget Message Budget Process The budget process takes nearly eight months. November — Town staff begin working on budget proposals for the next fiscal year, which starts July 1. January-March — The mayor and commissioners meet with key staff members in a retreat during the winter to plan for the budget. The retreat gives staff guidance on the priorities and direction for the budget. May — The town manager presents a recommended budget to the town board. The community is invited to review and share comments on the budget at a public hearing later in the month. The board also conducts a work session to discuss the budget at the same meeting. June — A second work session occurs in June if needed. The board votes on the budget at a regularly scheduled meeting in June. July — The approved budget goes into effect on July 1. Funds The town’s three budgetary funds are the General Fund, Stormwater Fund, and Water and Sewer Fund. General Fund — Primarily funded with property and sales tax revenues and pays for services like police protection, residential garbage collection and building and maintenance of recreation facilities. Stormwater and water and sewer funds — Enterprise funds that rely on user fees to pay for their specific services. No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. Strategic Plan The town uses a strategic plan to align available resources with priorities. The plan is used in coordination with the budget and the Comprehensive Sustainability Plan. Strategic Plan Comprehensive Sustainability Plan Financial Report Annual Financial Report The Annual Comprehensive Financial Report is a complete set of the town's financial statements for the previous fiscal year (July 1-June 30). The Financial Services Department oversees preparation of the annual report. The town's most recent annual financial report Past reports in the town's archives Volunteer Boards Budgets and Financial Statements Two of the town's appointed boards have activities supported by tax proceeds. Tourism Board activities are supported from a 1% tax on prepared food and beverages. Tourism Development Authority activities are supported from a 3% room occupancy tax. Budgets Tourism Board Budget Tourism Development Authority Budget Financial Statements Annual financial statements are prepared for the two boards. Water and sewer rates fund Hillsborough's water and sewer fund. This enterprise fund pays for providing water and sewer service, including operating, maintaining and improving the water and sewer system. The town's water and sewer rates are reviewed annually as part of the budget process. The rates schedule that is adopted with the budget becomes effective July 1 with the new fiscal year. Residents used an average of about 3,440 gallons of water and sewer monthly in the last fiscal year — July 2024 through June 2025. Fees and Charges Schedule About the Rates Minimum Usage Charge The town charges for a minimum amount of water and sewer use each month. Additional use for both is billed per 1,000 gallons. Providing the availability of water and sewer service costs the town money even if a customer uses no water that month. The costs include: Managing and protecting Hillsborough's water source. Treating, storing and distributing drinking water. Collecting, treating and returning used water to the environment. Managing water and sewer billing, including meter reading, billing software, and bill printing and mailing. These operations also have costs for qualified workers, facilities, vehicles, equipment, chemicals, electricity, gas, compliance with regulations, and administrative needs. Minimum Fee Versus Base Fee All water and sewer systems charge a base fee or a minimum usage fee to ensure they can provide service. Base fee — No water is provided  with the monthly charge. Minimum usage fee — Water is provided with the monthly charge up to the minimum number of gallons set. In-Town Versus Out-of-Town Rates The most recent published annual research in North Carolina shows a higher outside rate is charged by 98% of water systems and 95% of sewer systems that offer service outside their geographical limits. The annual study is provided by the UNC School of Government's Environmental Finance Center and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. Out-of-town water and sewer fees are higher than in-town fees due to: Cost of Service — The operations, maintenance, pumping of water and sewer, and reading of meters can be more costly in areas outside of town, mainly because of greater distance and travel time from the town’s water or wastewater treatment plants. Fewer customers in the area to spread the cost can make the service more expensive. Growth Management — P roviding water and sewer service to developments outside of town can cause the town to lose some ability to attract development that would improve its tax base, help improve the economies of scale in providing services, and potentially ease the tax burden on residents.  Lower in-town fees also can make annexation more desirable in areas that have water and sewer service. Investment Risk — In-town customers bear more of the investment risks of owning and operating the water and sewer system. When revenue bonds are issued to finance large projects, like the reservoir, the town pledges its full  rate setting authority for water and sewer as collateral to ensure the debt will be repaid. Equity — In-town customers bear more of the burden of financing and facilitating the water and sewer system’s operations. In addition to water and sewer payments, their municipal property taxes help pay for positions and resources that support the water and sewer operations. They also subsidize the use of Hillsborough services by out-of-town customers who live near the town’s corporate limits, including: Streets Police protection when inside town limits Recreation facilities, such as Gold Park, Kings Highway Park and the Riverwalk greenway Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System — Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply — The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management — A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades — $17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion — $10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion — $3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules — Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036, likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure — Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs. Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail. The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment — Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Videos Full Cost Pricing of Water Water: What You Pay For Understanding Enterprise Funds The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes. No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes primarily from water and sewer charges. Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated. Comparing Water and Sewer Rates Comparing rates is not simple, as the cost of service for each water and sewer system is based on a number of diverse factors. Customers should not expect a gallon of water to cost the same across water systems, notes an Environmental Finance Blog article . Annual Rates Research Annual rates research is conducted by the Environmental Finance Center at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. See: North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Dashboard North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Report Characteristics for Hillsborough Characteristics for Hillsborough that affect rates include that it: Is in the Central Pines Council of Government region. Is within the Upper Neuse River watershed and the Falls Lake watershed. Is affected by wastewater treatment mandates under the Falls Lake Rules, which are state mandates to protect and restore the water quality of Falls Lake. Charges for a minimum usage each billing cycle. Additional use is billed per 1,000 gallons. Charges out-of-town customers a rate that is 1.95 times the rate for in-town customers. Serves about 6,910 accounts and an estimated 13,995 people as of the start of Fiscal Year 2026. Had average residential customer use of about 3,440 gallons of water per month in the last fiscal year — July 2024 through June 2025. Water and sewer rates fund Hillsborough's water and sewer fund. This enterprise fund pays for providing water and sewer service, including operating, maintaining and improving the water and sewer system. The town's water and sewer rates are reviewed annually as part of the budget process. The rates schedule that is adopted with the budget becomes effective July 1 with the new fiscal year. Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated.

Comparing Water and Sewer Rates Comparing rates is not simple, as the cost of service for each water and sewer system is based on a number of diverse factors. Customers should not expect a gallon of water to cost the same across water systems, notes an Environmental Finance Blog article .

Annual Rates Research Annual rates research is conducted by the Environmental Finance Center at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. See: North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Dashboard North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Report Characteristics for Hillsborough Characteristics for Hillsborough that affect rates include that it: Is in the Central Pines Council of Government region. Is within the Upper Neuse River watershed and the Falls Lake watershed. Is affected by wastewater treatment mandates under the Falls Lake Rules, which are state mandates to protect and restore the water quality of Falls Lake. Charges for a minimum usage each billing cycle. Additional use is billed per 1,000 gallons. Charges out-of-town customers a rate that is 1.95 times the rate for in-town customers. Serves about 6,910 accounts and an estimated 13,995 people as of the start of Fiscal Year 2026. Had average residential customer use of about 3,440 gallons of water per month in the last fiscal year — July 2024 through June 2025.
