Checklist of Steps to Open a Business Below are steps that must be taken when starting, expanding or relocating a business. See the checklist for detailed information. Determine Zoning District ― Get help from economic development staff in determining the zoning district most appropriate for your business type before you sign a lease, purchase a building or enter into a land agreement. Select Possible Sites ― Economic development staff can suggest possible sites for your business. The town works closely with the Orange County Economic Development Office to keep an updated inventory of available sites. Meet about Development and Occupancy ― Hillsborough economic development staff will assist you in setting up a meeting with town and Orange County staff to discuss property development, new construction or the upfit of space to be leased. The town contracts with the county for building inspection services. Items to be discussed include: Compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, building codes and Health Department requirements for restaurants and food service establishments. Requirements for parking, lighting, landscaping, grading, grease traps, backflow prevention, fire access and fire suppression. Seek Permitting and Inspections ― Apply for all the permits necessary to start work on a space or to occupy the space once you've selected a site or existing building. These permits will have been discussed more extensively in the predevelopment meeting. Close the Project ― Economic development staff offer an optional meeting with business owners and developers at a project's conclusion to identify issues or challenges that may have impacted the project’s budget or timeline. The confidential meetings help improve the town's development processes. Business Checklist Inspections, Licenses and Permits Orange County Services The town contracts with Orange County for some services related to businesses, including building and fire inspections. Building Inspections and Permits — Contact the Orange County Building Inspections Division . Fire Inspection — All commercial spaces must have a fire safety inspection, which will determine if additional permits are needed. Contact the Orange County Fire Marshal Division . Town Services Requirements for businesses cross a number of areas, including planning, public works, stormwater and utilities. All businesses selling beer or wine must obtain a business license. Those providing prepared food and beverages or overnight stays must submit taxes. Permits the town issues include those for: Driveways Home occupation Historic District regulations Mobile food vendors New water or sewer connections Outdoor seating Property alterations Signage Special events Stormwater management and stream buffers See the following pages: Permits and Licenses Tax Rates and Compliance Utilities Operating Businesses within Homes Before starting a home business, contact the Hillsborough Planning and Economic Development Division to describe the business you plan to open. Staff will help determine if the type of business you want to open meets conditions for a zoning compliance permit . Frequently Asked Questions Starting a Home Occupation — Local Regulations Guide and Checklist

Fire Inspection — All commercial spaces must have a fire safety inspection, which will determine if additional permits are needed. Contact the Orange County Fire Marshal Division . Town Services Requirements for businesses cross a number of areas, including planning, public works, stormwater and utilities. All businesses selling beer or wine must obtain a business license. Those providing prepared food and beverages or overnight stays must submit taxes. Permits the town issues include those for: Driveways Home occupation Historic District regulations Mobile food vendors New water or sewer connections Outdoor seating Property alterations Signage Special events Stormwater management and stream buffers See the following pages: Permits and Licenses Tax Rates and Compliance Utilities

Step 1: Pre-development meetings — Town staff review the development process for the specific project and recommend how best to proceed. Step 2: Annexation or rezoning — The Hillsborough Planning Board reviews the proposed development and provides a recommendation to the Hillsborough Board of Commissioners, which then votes on the proposed development. Step 3: Public hearing — Some development activities are subject to public notice and public hearings. For items requiring public hearings, property owners within 500 feet of the subject property will receive a written notice. Additionally, signs are placed on the property indicating a public hearing is occurring and legal ads are posted in the local newspaper to inform residents.

This rendering shows South Churton Street with pedestrian improvements. It is from the South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study. The town often has major public projects that it is considering, planning or building. Projects are removed from this page after construction or the work is completed. The Public Space and Sustainability Division oversees most major town construction projects. See the Bid Postings page for bidding information on town projects. Town Projects Future Train Station — The town is working with the state to reestablish passenger rail service and provide for economic development in Hillsborough. It will use 20 acres that the town owns by the North Carolina Railroad corridor. See the Future Train Station project page. Lawndale Basin Sewer Rehabilitation — Sewer lines will be repaired and relined in the Lawndale Basin in northern Hillsborough. See the Lawndale Basin Sewer Rehabilitation project page. Parking Study — The town is comprehensively studying its parking supply and the demand for parking in the downtown area and West Hillsborough to address existing conditions and future needs. See the Parking Study page. Highway 86 North Facility — The facility will be renovated and storage outbuildings will be constructed to allow use by multiple town operations. See the Highway 86 North Facility project page. Ridgewalk Greenway — A feasibility study has been conducted on a future north-south greenway that would provide a bike/pedestrian route from the future train station to the downtown. See the Ridgewalk Greenway project page. River Pumping Station Relocation — The town is working to replace the 1970s-era River Pumping Station, moving it from the Eno River floodplain and increasing sewer capacity in the area. See the River Pumping Station Relocation project page. South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study — South Churton Street is being studied to develop design concepts that contribute to the town's character and meet the goals of a state-funded project to widen the road from Interstate 40 to the Eno River. See the South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study project page. Starfield Booster Pumping Station — The town is working to add a booster pump to improve the interconnection between the town's water supply and the Orange Water and Sewer Authority's supply in southern Orange County. See the Starfield Booster Pumping Station project page. Unified Development Ordinance Rewrite — The town is updating its unified development ordinance to ensure it aligns with the Hillsborough Comprehensive Sustainability Plan. See the Unified Development Ordinance Rewrite project page. Water and Sewer Facility Renovation and Expansion — The Adron F. Thompson Water/Sewer Facility will be renovated and expanded. See the Water and Sewer Facility Renovation and Expansion project page. Private Development Private Development Private development is not a public project undertaken by the town or any other municipality. The town does list large private development occurring or under review within town limits on the Development Projects page.

Hillsborough offers many opportunities for recreation. It does not provide recreational programming. Town-maintained parks and greenways are open daily from 7 a.m. until 30 minutes after sunset. The Public Space and Sustainability Division: Manages town parks and greenways. Implements decisions of the Parks and Recreation Board . Gold Park includes swings and other play equipment. Parks and Recreation Board Proposed Greenway Reservations Riverwalk Greenway Town Map Town Parks and Greenways See the Town Map for directions and amenities. Greenways Parks Calvin Street Greenway — This short greenway allows pedestrian and bicycle access from downtown and adjacent neighborhoods to Gold Park and Riverwalk. Riverwalk Greenway — This greenway along the Eno River connects Occoneechee Mountain State Natural Area and trails east of town. See the Riverwalk page. Community Parks Cates Creek Park — 1445 Cates Creek Parkway. This community park has a pollinator garden and a skate spot . The multi-use field with soccer goals can be reserved. See the Reservations and Bee City USA pages. Gold Park — 415 Dimmocks Mill Road. This community park includes a dog park and a part of the Riverwalk greenway. The multi-use field and large picnic shelter can be reserved. See the Reservations and Bee City USA pages. See the park's trail map . Natural Area Kings Highway Park — 1001 Ben Johnston Road. This natural area with trails is surrounded in part by Lake Ben Johnston. It includes a kayak launch . Neighborhood and Pocket Parks Hillsborough Heights Park — 510 Terrell Road Mayo Park — South side of West King Street Murray Street Park — 225 Murray St. Turnip Patch Park — 333 W. King St. Dog Park The dog park is a fenced-in area in Gold Park where dogs area allowed to be off leash. It has the same hours as Gold Park. Dog Park Rules Dogs must be: At least 6 months old and not in heat. Current on all required vaccinations, with rabies tags displayed. On a maximum 6-foot leash before entering and when leaving the facility. Within sight and under voice control of owner at all times. Immediately removed if showing aggression. Owners must: Collect and dispose of all dog waste. Help keep the off-leash area safe for all visitors. Report all dog bites to Orange County Animal Control. Call 919-942-7387 or 911. Ground Covering The dog park's ground is covered with shredded hardwood mulch that: Is safe for use by children and dogs. Helps reduce dust, dirt and the ground cover’s movement. Grass has not been planted because maintenance would be difficult with the park's heavy use and because establishing grass would require several seasons of nonuse. Self-Guided Walks Interpretive Signage The town's interpretive signage helps provide context to sites within Hillsborough. The signs provide understanding of the town's history, environment and culture. Most of the signs are along the Riverwalk greenway, but there are some at other key points of interest in town. See Signage Map Treasure Trees The Treasure Trees Program recognizes the preservation of significant trees in the Hillsborough area. Trees are selected by the Tree Board. View them on the Treasure Trees page. About Our Recreation No recreation is allowed at the town's reservoir. See the About Water page. Building in Flood-Prone Areas Undeveloped floodplains often serve the need for public parks and recreation. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. These recreation facilities require cleanup after a severe weather event to remove hazards. They are open for use many more days throughout the year than they are closed for weather and maintenance. Built to Withstand Flooding Riverwalk is located partially within Gold Park and was built along a sewer easement by the Eno River. Both recreation facilities were designed to withstand flooding. Gold Park’s dog park was built with a chain-link fence to allow floodwaters through. When flooding is expected, the fencing is left open to allow water carrying debris to flow through without damaging the fence. Severe Weather Parks and greenways are closed at times for the safety of the community and emergency workers. Hazards can exist even after a storm with high winds or snow has passed or after floodwaters have receded. Parks and greenways: Will remain closed following severe weather until the town can inspect the areas and remove any hazards. Are not a priority for snow and ice removal. They will be closed in deteriorating wintry conditions until staff determines they are safe. During Closures Heed any closure signs or barriers for parks and trails. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. By entering closed areas and by moving or removing closure signs and barriers, you also: Slow cleanup efforts and delay the reopening of parks and greenways for everyone. Force public works and public safety staff away from other duties to reinstall closure signs and barriers. Force emergency workers away from other duties for potential rescues. Risk the safety of others. Subscribe to town news and follow @HillsboroughGov social media to stay informed about closures and reopenings. Shade at Parks Town staff and appointed boards are working to provide shade options at parks. Fast-growing shade trees have been planted around the perimeter of Cates Creek and Gold parks. Trees are planted in available spaces around the play areas but not inside due to spacing regulations for the play equipment. Because of the play equipment's current height and spacing regulations, sunshades in the play areas would be installed at a height that would provide little sun blockage during the day and that would make the shades a safety risk in high winds. Water Fountains Water is turned off at exterior drinking fountains at parks when the first freezing temperatures of winter are forecasted. Water service then is restored in the spring when the danger of freezing temperatures has passed. This helps avoid water line breaks, which can be costly to repair and disruptive to the use of recreational areas. Make a Recommendation The Tree Board makes planting decisions for town spaces. Plants typically are added between November and March to improve survival due to less heat and more precipitation. The Parks and Recreation Board advises the town on recreation improvements and implements recommendations in recreation master plans. Other Recreation Recreation Offerings from Others Exchange Club Park is owned and maintained by the Exchange Club. Contact the club at 919-732-9283 for more information. Orange County provides recreational programming, a senior center and additional parks in and around town, including Fairview and River parks. See the county's: Trails and greenways map Parks locator map For other recreation options in the area and maps, visit the Hillsborough Visitors Center . Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9600 Office Hours — 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Shade at Parks Town staff and appointed boards are working to provide shade options at parks. Fast-growing shade trees have been planted around the perimeter of Cates Creek and Gold parks. Trees are planted in available spaces around the play areas but not inside due to spacing regulations for the play equipment. Because of the play equipment's current height and spacing regulations, sunshades in the play areas would be installed at a height that would provide little sun blockage during the day and that would make the shades a safety risk in high winds.

Water Fountains Water is turned off at exterior drinking fountains at parks when the first freezing temperatures of winter are forecasted. Water service then is restored in the spring when the danger of freezing temperatures has passed. This helps avoid water line breaks, which can be costly to repair and disruptive to the use of recreational areas.

Make a Recommendation The Tree Board makes planting decisions for town spaces. Plants typically are added between November and March to improve survival due to less heat and more precipitation. The Hillsborough Board of Commissioners is the town's legislative body and establishes policy and law. The board includes the mayor and five commissioners. The mayor votes only to break ties.

The board includes the mayor and five commissioners. The mayor votes only to break ties.

Elections and Terms The mayor and commissioners serve part time and are chosen through townwide nonpartisan elections. The mayor is elected to a two-year term. The five commissioners are elected to staggered four-year terms. Mayor and Commissioners See contact information, committee assignments and more for: Mayor Mark Bell Mayor Pro Tem Meaghun Darab Commissioner Robb English Commissioner Kathleen Ferguson Commissioner Matt Hughes Commissioner Evelyn P. Course Basics The multiweek course is coordinated by the Communications Division and includes time with the Administrative Services, Community Services, Financial Services, Police and Utilities departments and  staff from the following divisions and operations: Budget Fleet Maintenance Planning and Economic Development Public Space and Sustainability Public Works Stormwater and Environmental Services Tours Visits are made to: A fire station in Hillsborough to learn about the Orange Rural Fire Department and the Hillsborough Police Department. The town contracts with Orange Rural for fire service. The town's water and sewer facilities, including the water and wastewater treatment plants. Program Goals The program is intended to: Increase community members' knowledge, interest and ability to influence and participate in town government. Reduce barriers to participating in and influencing town government decisions. This occurs through increased understanding of key processes and operations of the town. Assist in building community members' interest in serving the community through future service. Learn from community members attending the course.

The Hillsborough Police Department is responsible for general public safety and for enforcing state laws and town ordinances within town limits. The department serves all people  with respect, fairness and dignity through: Community-based policing Building partnerships Problem-solving Fair and impartial policing
The Hillsborough Police Department is responsible for general public safety and for enforcing state laws and town ordinances within town limits.
Welcome to the Hillsborough Police Department in Hillsborough, North Carolina
I am honored to serve the Hillsborough community alongside a dedicated team of professionals who deeply care about the people we protect.
The department commits to excellence through our core values:
Community — Beyond just serving the community, the Police Department is an active member of the community and will contribute and engage.

Mission and Vision Mission The Hillsborough Police Department exists to make the Hillsborough vision a reality by providing exceptional service, protecting and enhancing our community, helping those in need, and exemplifying policing at its best through the most positive and professional law enforcement services possible. Vision The Hillsborough Police Department will be an example of the very best law enforcement service possible and will always strive to exceed our community’s expectations through our character, operations, interactions and personnel.

The Hillsborough Police Department is organized into two divisions, each led by a captain.
Serves as the front line protecting the community.
Request an officer to appear at an event.

Internal Accountability The Police Department relies on an internal review process for accountability to policies, laws and best practices for the department's work. The internal review process addresses external complaints as well as incidents flagged internally for review. Every effort is made to investigate complaints from the community as completely as possible from the information provided. Example Internal Review Use of force is an example of an incident that may trigger an internal review. Department policy requires that use of force is reported for the display of a weapon toward a person and for any action that goes beyond the reasonably expected use of soft hands for guidance when detaining a person. Each incident of reported use of force is reviewed at several levels. A review generally includes: Viewing body worn camera footage. Reviewing statements of the officers, witnesses and suspects involved. Assessing any other possible evidence.

Ice and Snow Removal Pre-treatment If needed, the town will pretreat the following roadways with brine: A-E — Beckett’s Ridge Drive, Cameron Street, Cates Creek Parkway, College Park Road, East Corbin Street and Exchange Park Lane F-J — Hampton Pointe Boulevard and John Earl Street K-O — Lafayette Drive, Lakeshore Drive, East Margaret Lane, Meadowlands Drive and Millstone Drive R-Z — Rainey Avenue and Waterstone Drive Plowing Guidelines The town plows roadways to provide emergency responders with safe, timely access. Public Works employees monitor snowfall on pavement. Employees plow all town-maintained streets. They first plow town bridges, emergency services and the Orange County refueling station within the town limits. During heavy snow, employees work to keep priority areas clear. They plow the remaining town-maintained streets when weather conditions allow. Plowing is stopped if it does not clear roads or if it makes conditions worse. Responsibilities Roads and Driveways The state owns and maintains the main roads in town. The town does not clear driveways after plowing and does not maintain private streets. Parks, Greenways and Sidewalks Parks, greenways and sidewalks are not a priority for snow removal. The town will clear sidewalks around town facilities when time allows. The town does not remove snow or ice from residential sidewalks in town. Property and business owners are responsible for snow and ice removal from sidewalks in front of structures they own or occupy. More Information Cold Weather Resources Ready Hillsborough Services for Your Property Snow and Ice Removal Map Winter Weather Operations video Removal of Animals Struck by Vehicles Public Works employees remove animals that have been struck by vehicles from town-maintained roads, including rights of way. These roads must be in the town limits. The town does not remove animals from private property. Report a dead animal by placing a work order or calling the administrative support specialist. Call 911 to report conditions that pose an immediate threat to safety. To report a dead animal on a state road, use the state's contact form or call 877-368-4968. Street Repairs and Repaving Report potholes and other damage to town-maintained streets through the Public Works contact form or by calling the administrative support specialist. Provide the specific location of the problem, such as the nearest street address or intersection. Call 911 to report conditions that pose an immediate threat to safety. Repaving Hillsborough repaves streets about every 20 years. Annually, it tries to repave about 5% of its roads with town and state funds. Staff pick streets based on their condition and age. About every five years, the town pays an engineering firm to analyze the condition of town-maintained roads. Some roads may be sealed or patched to extend their use. Street Repaving Video Street and Traffic Signs Street and traffic signs are installed and maintained by the Public Works Division. Posters or other signage may not be placed on any sign or post. Report problems through the Public Works contact form or by calling the administrative support specialist.

The Public Works Division provides weekly household garbage collection and monthly bulk items collection for single-family homes.

Curbside Collections Calendar A brochure with the calendar is mailed annually to each resident who receives town solid waste services. Collection days and holiday changes are only for the town's collection of garbage and yard waste. For information on yard waste, including loose leaves, visit the Yard Waste Collection page. For information on recycling, visit the Orange County website . Curbside Collections Calendar
A brochure with the calendar is mailed annually to each resident who receives town solid waste services.
Find your street or neighborhood below to determine your collection day.
Collection is on regular garbage collection days for most Hillsborough streets.

Bulk Items The monthly collection occurs the second full week of the month. Sunday is considered the start of the week. See the curbside collections calendar for exact weeks. Bulk items may be placed by the street up to seven calendar days before the scheduled collection. The collection must fit into a pickup truck bed. A fee is assessed for items exceeding the typical load or for collection outside the regular collection week. See the special collections section. Bulk Items Collected Appliances Furniture Grills, if clean and free of charcoal and fuel tanks Mattresses Scrap metal, if clean Unmounted (rimless) tires Water heaters For questions about other items, contact the Public Works Division. Garbage Household garbage collection is provided once a week to single-family homes. Use town-provided carts. Each household is allowed up to two carts. For a second cart, see the sections on rollout carts and fees. Follow town requirements for when and how carts may be placed. See the section on rollout carts. Place only bagged household garbage in rollout carts. Dispose paint cans with household garbage if they are dried, flattened and lidless. Completely close the cart lid. Remove all rollout carts and uncollected trash from the street by 6 a.m. the day after collection. Garbage Items Not Collected The town does not collect the items below. See Orange County's A to Z Waste and Recycling Guide and Solid Waste Management website for how to recycle or dispose of these items. Batteries Cardboard (must be recycled) Chemical byproducts Construction or demolition materials (includes lumber) Fuel cans or tanks Hazardous waste Medical waste Plastic bottles other than oil bottles (must be recycled) Pressurized containers Radioactive waste Shock sensitive waste Used oil Special Collections Garbage Collection for Residents with Disabilities The town provides free, special garbage collection for residents with disabilities who live alone or have no able-bodied person in the household. Public Works employees retrieve the rollout trash carts from the homes of those who qualify. To qualify, a doctor must provide written confirmation that the individual cannot move the rollout cart. To continue the service, a doctor’s letter must be provided yearly by May 1. The town will not request personal or health information. For more information or to apply, contact the administrative support specialist. Special Bulk Items Collection Special collection of bulk items includes: Items that exceed a typical load. Collection outside of the regular collection week. The special collection requires a work order and fee. The fee begins at $50 and is based on the nature and amount of materials and on the estimated time required to collect them. Fees must be paid before collection is scheduled. Submit a work order or call the administrative support specialist.

Commercial Waste Independent vendors provide trash collection to multi-family housing communities, businesses, churches and nonprofits. Businesses may use any vendor they choose if town standards are met. See the regulations and an application for a dumpster exemption . Food Waste Composting food waste reduces disposal fees the town must pay and benefits the environment. Drop-off Sites Food waste can be dropped off for free at 11 sites in Orange County, including: Eno River Farmers Market in downtown Hillsborough. Orange County 24-hour recycling drop-off site in Hampton Pointe. Orange County waste and recycling center on Walnut Grove Church Road, north of Hillsborough. The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality adds public and private composting sites to the NC Food Donation and Compost Resource Map as they become available. Acceptable Items Items that can be dropped off for composting at these sites includes: Food scraps. Food-related paper, including napkins, greasy pizza boxes and staple-free tea bags. Flowers and trimmings from small indoor plants. For more information on the drop-off sites and a comprehensive list of acceptable items, see the Orange County food waste page. Composting at Home Composting can be done at home and can reduce fertilizer, herbicide and water needs for plants. Learn how to get started on the Orange County Cooperative Extension website . For those without the space, conditions or desire for backyard composting, countertop composters can be purchased to provide a soil additive with less effort. Countertop composters may use a combination of grinders and heat to reduce food volume and create compost. Fees and Violations Fees can be paid several ways. Contact the administrative support specialist for more information. Cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Credit or debit card through the town's payments and donations website . Violations If requirements for collection are not followed, the garbage or bulk items will not be collected. An employee will leave information on the town's policy for the resident. If trash carts and uncollected garbage are not removed from the street as required, a notice of violation will be delivered to the resident by mail or by town staff. A fee of $25 per day will be assessed if the resident does not comply with the ordinance within five days of the notice date. Recycling and Other Solid Waste Services Orange County collects recycling at curbs on Wednesdays in Hillsborough. See the county's Urban and Town Curbside (Weekly Collection) page for requirements. The county also provides other trash services to Hillsborough residents. Contact Orange County Solid Waste Management for these services, including: Recycling drop-off sites. Household hazardous waste collection. Construction and demolition waste collection. Solid waste convenience centers. Rollout Carts For collection and safety, carts must be: Placed at the curb between 4 p.m. the day before collection and 6:30 a.m. the day of collection. Placed within 3 feet of the curb, with the handle facing away from the street. Placed at least 4 feet from parked cars, trees or other obstructions. Removed from the street by 6 a.m. the day after collection. See our video Position Your Solid Waste and Recycling Carts Properly . Rollout Cart Delivery and Repair Residents in single-family homes with active utility accounts may receive one free trash rollout cart after account activation if they live in town limits on a publicly owned road. See the Services for Your Property page to determine whether a property is in town limits. A fee is charged for a second cart. Payment must be received before delivery is scheduled. See the fees section for how to pay the fee. Place a work order or call the administrative support specialist to request: Cart delivery or repair Second cart
Businesses may use any vendor they choose if town standards are met. See the regulations and an application for a dumpster exemption .

The Public Works Division strives to maintain a high quality of life for residents by providing a safe, clean and healthy environment in town. The division: Provides collection of residential garbage and yard waste. Maintains town-owned streets and stormwater drainage areas. Oversees cemetery operations and burials at the Town Cemetery.
The Public Works Division strives to maintain a high quality of life for residents by providing a safe, clean and healthy environment in town. The division:
Provides collection of residential garbage and yard waste.
Oversees cemetery operations and burials at the Town Cemetery.

The Public Works Division collects yard waste weekly for single-family homes. Loose leaves are collected November through January. Keep leaves out of streets and stormwater ditches. Collected yard waste is used for composting and mulching. Curbside Collections Calendar
Keep leaves out of streets and stormwater ditches.
Collected yard waste is used for composting and mulching.

Bagged Leaves and Grass The town collects leaves and grass clippings year-round in paper or biodegradable bags. Collection occurs on brush collection days. Up to 10 bags may be collected per household each week. Requirements Keep soil, trash and debris out of bags. Place bags: Within 4 feet of the curb but not on the curb. Where they do not interfere with traffic or water drainage. Where they do not block sidewalks, fire hydrants or water meters. Brush The town collects woody material from trees and shrubs weekly as part of its brush collection. Most homes receive collection on their regular garbage collection days. Homes on the following streets receive collection on Wednesday: Lydia and East Margaret lanes Streets including Caine, North and South Cameron, East Corbin, Huddle, East King, Mitchell, East Orange, East Queen, East Tryon, and East Union. Requirements Keep trash and loose leaves out of brush. Ensure brush is no larger than 6 feet in length and 6 inches in diameter. Place brush within 4 feet of the curb, 3 feet away from obstructions and not below trees or power lines. Stack limbs in one pile, with all limbs in the same direction. Place small amounts of brush in biodegradable paper bags. Or tie small amounts into bundles weighing less than 30 pounds. Use jute or sisal twine for the bundles. Do not use plastic or cotton twine. Allow a bagful or several armfuls of sticks to accumulate before placing at the curb. Christmas trees can be collected with brush with restrictions. See the Additional Information section. Loose Leaves The town collects loose leaves November through January. Collection occurs once per week, depending on the amount of leaves and weather. Collection does not occur on town-observed holidays. Requirements Keep trash, sticks and other debris out of loose leaves and bags. Place loose leaves and bags: Within 4 feet of the curb but not on the curb. Where they do not interfere with traffic or water drainage. Where they do not block sidewalks, fire hydrants or water meters.

Christmas Trees The town collects Christmas trees with brush collection. Trees must be: Removed from the stand. Free of tinsel, ornaments, lights and other decorations. Trees also may be brought to any of Orange County’s waste and recycling centers except the center on Bradshaw Quarry Road. Delivery of Bulk Leaves From November through January, public works employees will deliver leaves to those within an 8-mile radius of Hillsborough who would like the leaves for mulch or compost. Priority is given to Hillsborough residents. The minimum amount is half a truckload, or about 10 cubic yards. The delivery area must be accessible by a large truck. To request a delivery, submit a work order or call the administrative support specialist. Special Collections, Fees and Violations Special Collections, Violations and Fees Special collections of leaves or brush outside of normal collection days require a work order and a fee. The minimum fee is $130. Fees must be paid before collection is scheduled. Submit a work order or call the administrative support specialist. Fees Fees can be paid several ways. Contact the administrative support specialist for more information. Cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Do not place cash in the deposit box at the back of the building. Credit or debit card through the town's payments and donations website . Violations If yard waste does not comply with requirements, it will not be collected. An employee will leave information on the town's policy for the resident. The town may halt irrigation service during water shortages. If service is halted, the town will bill for the amount of water used during the affected months. It will not charge a minimum fee during those months.

Bill Adjustments for Leaks Customers may request an adjustment to their bills for leaks caused by water line breaks. One leak adjustment is allowed in a 12-month period. Account adjustments are not usually made for irrigation leaks. Billing and Adjustments Check for Leaks Fix leaks to save on utility bills. Nationally, 10% of homes have leaks of 90 gallons or more per day. Over a month, that's at least 2,700 gallons. A small drip from a worn washer can waste 20 or more gallons daily. Large leaks can waste hundreds of gallons daily. Check Around the House Check faucets and pipes. Replace worn washers immediately. Listen carefully for running water. Look for wet spots in your yard between the water meter and house. Regularly check your irrigation system for leaks. Check Your Bill A family of four's usage should be under 12,000 gallons in winter months. Look for significant changes from one month to the next.

In the Bathroom Reduce the amount of water used: Wet your toothbrush and then turn off the faucet. Rinse your razor in a partially filled sink instead of under a running tap. Shorten your shower. Cutting out one or two minutes can save up to 700 gallons of water each month. Take a shallow bath. This saves more water than a short shower. Install low-flow showerheads or flow restrictors. Install ultra-low flush toilets and faucets if building or remodeling a home. Reduce the amount of water flushed: Flush only when necessary and put trash in the trash can. Each flush wastes 5 to 7 gallons of water, and flushed trash can clog pipes. Put a filled plastic bottle in your toilet tank. Fill the bottle with water and a small amount of pebbles to weigh it down. Place it away from operating mechanisms. This can save you 10 or more gallons of water each day. Save water for other use: Place a bucket under the bath faucet when waiting for water to warm. Use the retained cold water later for house plants or to refill the toilet tank after flushing. Use water from pet dishes or dehumidifiers to water plants. In the Kitchen and Laundry Reduce the use of running water: Keep water in the refrigerator for drinking instead of running the faucet until water gets cold. Rinse vegetables in a pan of water. Defrost frozen foods in the microwave or refrigerator. Use the garbage disposal less. Consider composting. Putting food scraps and grease and oils down kitchen sinks risks clogging sewer pipes. Fill one sink with soapy water and one with rinse water when washing dishes by hand. If you have only one sink, use a dish rack and rinse with hot water. Use the smallest amount of detergent possible when washing dishes by hand. This reduces the amount of rinse water needed and the risk of clogged pipes. Use your dishwasher and washing machine for full loads only. If considering the replacement of large appliances: Select high-efficiency washing machines. Consider that new dishwasher models are more water- and energy-efficient. Outside and Dining Out If possible for outside plantings: Reduce or eliminate watering grass, bushes and plants. Reduce or eliminate planting new plants. If possible for outside maintenance: Avoid washing vehicles at home. Most commercial car washes recycle the wash water. Soapy runoff is not good for the environment or stormwater system. Consider delaying projects that use a lot of water, such as: Carpet cleaning with your own steam cleaner. Draining and refilling a hot water heater. Filling ponds, hot tubs, fountains or pools. Masonry projects that need a water mix for grout or concrete. Painting projects that require washing brushes, rollers and trays. Power washing. When dining out, if you: Won’t be drinking water, ask that no tap water be served. Will be drinking water, ask for bottled water during water restrictions.

Keep water in the refrigerator for drinking instead of running the faucet until water gets cold.
Rinse vegetables in a pan of water.
Defrost frozen foods in the microwave or refrigerator.
Use the garbage disposal less. Consider composting. Putting food scraps and grease and oils down kitchen sinks risks clogging sewer pipes.
Fill one sink with soapy water and one with rinse water when washing dishes by hand. If you have only one sink, use a dish rack and rinse with hot water.
Use the smallest amount of detergent possible when washing dishes by hand. This reduces the amount of rinse water needed and the risk of clogged pipes.
Use your dishwasher and washing machine for full loads only.
If considering the replacement of large appliances:
Avoid washing vehicles at home. Most commercial car washes recycle the wash water. Soapy runoff is not good for the environment or stormwater system.
Draining and refilling a hot water heater.
Masonry projects that need a water mix for grout or concrete.

Policy The mayor can issue a proclamation to restrict water use when water is in short supply in the Eno River, Lake Orange or the town's reservoir. The level of restrictions is determined by the available water supply. Construction-related activities that are not essential and that require water should not be undertaken during a declared water shortage. See the full drought policy . Stage 1: Voluntary Restrictions This stage begins when the town has less than 180 days of supply. Customers are encouraged in this stage to reduce water use by 10%. The town also may: Offer water-saving devices. Provide daily or weekly reservoir level and water usage statistics. Recommend water usage reduction techniques. Stage 2: Mandatory Restrictions This stage begins when the town has less than 135 days of supply. Customers must reduce water use by 10% and are encouraged to reduce use by 20%. No pressure washing or private car washing is allowed. Outdoor water use is limited to two days per week. Properties with odd-numbered street addresses may water on Tuesday and Saturday. Even-numbered properties may water on Wednesday and Sunday. The town must: Offer water-saving devices. Provide daily or weekly reservoir level and water usage statistics. Recommend water usage reduction techniques. Stage 3: Rationing This stage begins when the town has less than 90 days of supply. All customers must reduce water use by 30%, except for special medical use. Customers are not required to use less than the minimum usage billed. No outdoor water usage is allowed, other than for emergency use or fire protection. Irrigation water meters will be turned off or removed by the town. The town will: Lower water pressure in its distribution system by reducing tank storage levels to the extent possible. Capture any water flushed from water lines for water quality concerns and return it to the Eno River upstream of the town's raw water intake. Use other water sources if feasible, investigating such sources as rock quarries, ponds, springs, and other utility systems. Enhance public awareness of the daily water usage, reservoir level and goal for water usage.

Subscribe to news releases or sign up for emergency alerts . Utility Easement Mowing to Begin Before you plant or build, check for easements on your property. Post Date: May 15, 2026 8:00 a.m. Utilities easements are periodically mowed and cleared of brush to keep them accessible for maintenance and repairs. A contractor for the town is expected to begin mowing water and sewer easements on Monday, May 18. The work is expected to be finished by the end of June. Two crews from Wellsmont Landscaping will work simultaneously. One will start in the northern service area by a town water tank near Pathways Elementary School and the other in the southern zone near another town water tank in the Waterstone development. They will work toward Hillsborough’s central service area. Please stay alert for mowing crews for your safety and the safety of workers. About Easements Easements are documented to reserve property for a specific use, such as maintaining the town's water and sewer system. They do not remove property from an owner, but they must be kept clear of any plantings or structures. Before you plant or build, make sure you are aware of any easements on your property. If utilities workers need to access a town easement, the town will not replace any plants, fences, sheds or other structures that are removed from the easement. The town also cannot plant or maintain any trees or bushes within its easements. More Information To learn more about the responsibilities of property owners with easements and those of other customers who have utilities features within an easement or right of way, see the Easements section on the Utilities page. Two crews from Wellsmont Landscaping will work simultaneously. One will start in the northern service area by a town water tank near Pathways Elementary School and the other in the southern zone near another town water tank in the Waterstone development. They will work toward Hillsborough’s central service area.
Please stay alert for mowing crews for your safety and the safety of workers.

Easements are documented to reserve property for a specific use, such as maintaining the town's water and sewer system. They do not remove property from an owner, but they must be kept clear of any plantings or structures.

Before you plant or build, make sure you are aware of any easements on your property. If utilities workers need to access a town easement, the town will not replace any plants, fences, sheds or other structures that are removed from the easement.
The town also cannot plant or maintain any trees or bushes within its easements.

To learn more about the responsibilities of property owners with easements and those of other customers who have utilities features within an easement or right of way, see the Easements section on the Utilities page.

Subscribe to news releases or sign up for emergency alerts . Keep Wipes and Other Trash Out of Pipes So-called flushable wipes do not break down in the sewer system. Post Date: May 13, 2026 8:00 a.m. Entangled wipes, plastics and other trash made this sewer pump at the Elizabeth Brady Pump Station inoperable in 2023, requiring replacement. Each pump costs about $34,000 and helps transport sewage to the Wastewater Treatment Plant. Did you know that wipes and other personal hygiene and cleaning products are the leading cause of clogged pipes? Only waste and toilet paper should be flushed. Items to Avoid Flushing Household cleaning and personal hygiene products labeled as flushable or safe for sewer or septic systems do not dissolve. They should always be placed in the trash. If your house is professionally cleaned, please ask the cleaning company to put these items in the trash: Floor cleaning pads, such as Swiffer wet or dry mopping cloths Disinfecting wipes Paper towels In addition to cleaning products, throw away the following: Tampons and pads Facial tissues Personal wipes Medicine These items and other trash clog sewer lines and can damage sewer pumps and other equipment. Costly Repairs Blocked pipes can lead to sewer overflows, which can be costly for you and the town’s utilities department. Consider the responsibilities: Your responsibility — Property owners are responsible for blockages in the sewer service line between the house or structure on their property and the public right of way or easement. The town can require installation or relocation of a cleanout at the right of way or easement if one is not present. Shared responsibility — The town owns and maintains the sewer main or sewer service line in public rights of ways or easements. It will clear blockages of lines in public rights of ways or easements. The town may require property owners to install or relocate cleanouts to the town's specifications. The town may agree to perform the work but may require reimbursement and the granting of property access from the customer. More Information See the town’s No Wipes in the Pipes video. For more information, contact the utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. Did you know that wipes and other personal hygiene and cleaning products are the leading cause of clogged pipes? Hillsborough Wastewater Plant Operator III Shawn Maines will retire June 1 after over 28 years with the town.

Maines began his career with the Utilities Department in April 1998. He quickly became certified as a wastewater treatment plant operator I and later gained additional credentials and responsibilities. He qualified as a plant operator II in 2008 and as operator III the following year. In 2013, he became the town’s chief wastewater plant operator and served in that role until last year. Maines credits Mahagan with encouraging him to achieve the additional certifications.

As a plant operator, Maines has run the plant, which includes startup and shutdown processes, testing the treated wastewater before it flows to the Eno River, and operating the belt press that removes solids from wastewater near the head of the plant. The work has included many overnight and weekend shifts and keeping the plant operational through ice storms and other inclement weather.
The town is accepting applications for a police officer and a municipal sanitation and equipment operator. See the Job Opportunities website. Also view the town’s recruitment video .

A right of way or easement gives legal right for public uses or to perform work on infrastructure. They are documented to reserve property for the specific use, such as maintaining the town's water and sewer system. Easements do not remove property from owners, but they must be kept clear of any plantings or structures. Town Responsibilities The town periodically mows water and sewer easements to keep them accessible for routine maintenance and emergency response related to town utility assets. The mowing typically occurs in the spring and fall and includes the removal of brush and undergrowth. The town will not replace any plants, fences, sheds or other structures that are removed from a utilities easement when utilities workers need to access the easement. The town is responsible for any repairs to property damaged by heavy equipment used in the clearing. Customer Responsibilities Awareness Property owners with utilities easements need to be aware of the easement boundaries before building or planting. Contact the Engineering Services Division to: Learn if you have an easement on your property. Request permission before placing anything in an easement. Upkeep and Clearances Property owners are responsible for upkeep, such as mowing, of their property where easements are located and within the road right of way. Property owners also must maintain sufficient clearance to allow the town to access, operate and maintain its assets. C learance requirements are measured from the center point (radius) of the device: Fire hydrants — 3 feet Manholes and concrete vaults — 6 feet Water meters, valve boxes, sewer cleanouts and other utilities features not listed — 2 feet Additional information on the technical specifications for utilities and sanitary sewer use can be found in the Hillsborough Code of Ordinances , chapters 14 and 15. Contact Information Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. 919-296-9630 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Engineering Services Staff Title Phone Email Green, Bryant Engineering Services Manager 919-296-9630 Email

The Utilities Department prepares water for drinking and cleans used water for return to the environment. It also maintains pipes and infrastructure to deliver drinking water and transport wastewater for treatment. See the Water and Sewer Services page for billing, rates, service connection and other services related to the water and sewer system. West Fork Eno Reservoir The Eno River is Hillsborough's source for water. No recreation is allowed at the reservoir. Learn More Tour our water plant See what it takes to prepare water from the Eno River for drinking. Sign Up Tour our wastewater plant What happens to the water you use? See how the town cleans the water for return to the Eno River. Sign Up Annual Reports Hillsborough's treatment plants regularly test for contaminants and prepare annual reports about their treatment processes and testing. Water Quality Report An annual report is published in July for the previous year. It includes  information on our water treatment system and performance as well as water quality information from the Environmental Protection Agency. Water Quality Report Wastewater Quality Report A report is published in August each year for the previous fiscal year (July 1-June 30). It includes information on our wastewater collection and treatment systems and their performance. Wastewater Quality Report Distribution and Collection Division The Water Distribution and Wastewater Collection Division: Repairs water line leaks. Cleans and repairs sewer lines. Maintains water tanks, booster pump stations and sewage lift stations. Staff members are responsible for programs and projects to maintain and protect the utility system. See: About Water About Wastewater Treatment Plants The town’s treatment plants prepare drinking water and clean used water. They regularly test for contaminants. Water Treatment Plant The water plant takes water from the Eno River, cleans and disinfects it, and then pumps it to customers. The state requires that the plant change its disinfection method for one month each year to clean the distribution system. About Water Wastewater Treatment Plant The wastewater plant removes waste and nutrients from used water, disinfects the water with chlorine, and then removes the chlorine before releasing the water into the Eno River. Corbin St. 919-296-9630 Office Hours — 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Tampering or Theft — Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form.
